timeline.event: Born as the second son of 8 children in Ajaccio, Corsica.
Went to France with his older brother Joseph and entered the religious school attached to the Autun Abbey.
Entered the military school of Brienne.
Entered the Ecole Militaire in Paris.
His father Carlo died of stomach cancer.
Passed the artillery officer appointment exam and was commissioned as a second lieutenant at the age of 16.
Assigned as an artillery officer to the La Fere regiment stationed in Valence.
Worked there when the La Fere regiment moved its garrison to Auxonne.
The French Revolution broke out.
Took a leave and returned to his hometown of Corsica.
Louis XVI was executed.
Detained by Paoli's side, his house and farm were looted, and he escaped Corsica with his family.
Won by recapturing the L'Eguillette and Balaguier forts at the Siege of Toulon and was promoted to brigadier general.
The Thermidorian Reaction occurred, and Robespierre's group was executed.
Imprisoned due to his friendship with Robespierre's younger brother Augustin but soon released.
Suppressed the royalist riot in Paris.
Married Josephine de Beauharnais.
Appointed commander of the Army of Italy.
Occupied the Austrian capital of Vienna and signed the Treaty of Campo Formio.
Landed at the port of Alexandria in Egypt with about 50,000 troops, beginning the Egyptian campaign.
The French navy was defeated by Horatio Nelson's British fleet at the Battle of Aboukir Bay.
Escaped from Egypt and returned to France.
Staged the Coup of 18 Brumaire with the revolutionary government's power figure Sieyes.
A new constitution establishing three consuls was submitted to a national referendum and passed.
The French expeditionary force crossed the Great St Bernard Pass.
Achieved victory at the Battle of Marengo against the Austrian army.
Established the Bank of France to promote economic stability.
Amicably settled relations with the United States through the Treaty of Mortefontaine.
Signed the Concordat of 1801 with Pope Pius VII.
Sent an expeditionary force of about 30,000 to Haiti (Saint-Domingue) in an attempt to recapture the colony and revive slavery.
Signed the Treaty of Amiens with Britain, achieving stability after 10 years.
Created the Legion of Honour.
Became First Consul for life, strengthening his dictatorial power.
Sold Louisiana to the United States for $15 million.
Discovered the assassination plot by Georges Cadoudal and royalists who opposed Napoleon and hoped for a Bourbon restoration.
Gave up the occupation of Haiti and withdrew troops.
Haiti formally became independent, becoming the first black republic.
The acceptance of the empire was decided through a national referendum.
Held his coronation at Notre-Dame Cathedral, becoming Napoleon I, the first Emperor of the French Empire.
The French navy was devastatingly defeated by the British navy led by Nelson at the Battle of Trafalgar.
The French army won a decisive victory at the Battle of Austerlitz, and Austria surrendered by signing the Treaty of Pressburg.
Placed his younger brother Louis on the throne of Holland.
Defeated the Prussian army at the Battle of Jena-Auerstedt and entered Berlin.
Issued the Continental System through the Berlin Decree.
Signed the Treaties of Tilsit with Alexander I of Russia.
Invaded Portugal when it violated the Continental System.
Occupied Rome.
The residents of Madrid started a riot against the French army.
Annexed the Papal States.
Arrested the Pope and imprisoned him in Savona after he declared excommunication.
Divorced Empress Josephine.
Remarried Marie Louise, daughter of Austrian Emperor Francis I.
Sweden offered the Swedish succession to French Marshal Bernadotte.
Marie Louise gave birth to Napoleon II, King of Rome.
Conducted the Russian campaign leading an army of 600,000.
Forcibly transferred the Pope to Fontainebleau.
The French army won the large battle near Borodino.
Occupied Moscow.
The French army retreated from Russia.
Signed the Concordat of Fontainebleau with Pope Pius VII.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the allied forces at the Battle of Leipzig.
The French capital of Paris fell.
Signed the Treaty of Fontainebleau and was exiled as the sovereign of Elba.
Escaped from Elba.
Entered Paris and was restored as Emperor of the French.
Defeated by the combined attack of Britain and Prussia at the Battle of Waterloo, ending the Hundred Days.
Exiled to Saint Helena in the South Atlantic.
Passed away at 5:49 PM.
His remains were returned to France and entombed at Les Invalides in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Enactment of the Napoleonic Code
keyAchievements.description: Enacted the French Civil Code, the so-called Napoleonic Code, in 1804, which greatly influenced civil law worldwide.

keyAchievements.title: Development of military tactics and systems
keyAchievements.description: Influenced modern warfare militarily and developed tactics, strategy, training, organization, logistics, clothing, and reward systems.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Siege of Toulon
keyAchievements.description: After leading the victory at the Siege of Toulon in 1793, he was promoted to brigadier general at the age of 24.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Italian campaign
keyAchievements.description: Achieved great success in the Italian campaign in 1796, winning consecutive victories in the War of the First Coalition, and rode a wave of success as a soldier.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Bank of France
keyAchievements.description: Established the Bank of France in 1800 to promote economic stability.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Legion of Honour
keyAchievements.description: Created the famous Legion of Honour in 1802.

keyAchievements.title: Concordat of 1801
keyAchievements.description: Signed a political and religious concordat with Pope Pius VII in 1801, seeking reconciliation with the Roman Catholic Church and recognizing Protestants.

keyAchievements.title: Paris redevelopment project
keyAchievements.description: Promoted modernization policies for the city to make Paris, the capital of France, the world's foremost capital after becoming emperor.

faq.question: Was Napoleon actually short?
faq.answer: No, his actual height was 167-168cm, which was taller than the average height of 164cm for French soldiers in their 20s at the time. The misconception that he was short is due to differences in units of measurement and confusion with Napoleon III.

faq.question: How did Napoleon die?
faq.answer: His official cause of death is known as stomach cancer, but a large amount of arsenic was detected in his hair, raising theories of poisoning.

faq.question: Was Beethoven's Symphony 'Eroica' dedicated to Napoleon?
faq.answer: Beethoven considered Napoleon a hero of republicanism and named his Symphony No. 3 'Bonaparte', but was disappointed when Napoleon became emperor, abandoned the dedication, and changed the title to 'Eroica'.

faq.question: What started Napoleon's downfall?
faq.answer: His downfall began due to the lack of goods and daily necessities caused by the Continental System in 1806, the failure to occupy the Iberian Peninsula, and the failure of the Russian campaign in 1812.

timeline.event: Born in Junsu-bang, Hanseong, as the third son of King Taejong and Queen Wongyeong.
Invested as the new Crown Prince after his older brother, Grand Prince Yangnyeong, was deposed.
Ascended to the throne as the 4th King of Joseon at Geunjeongjeon in Gyeongbokgung Palace following Taejong's abdication.
Appointed Yi Jong-mu as the Commander-in-Chief of the Three Provinces to lead 227 warships and 17,000 soldiers to conquer Tsushima Island.
Established the Hall of Worthies (Jiphyeonjeon) inside the palace as a policy research institute to operate the centralized system.
Exempted from the tribute of virgins and gold/silver to the Ming Dynasty.
Troops led by Kim Jong-seo swept the Jurchens in the Hamgil province and established the Six Garrisons to pioneer the northern territory.
Personally created 28 letters out of pity for the common people who could not easily learn Chinese characters, the writing system at the time.
Promulgated Hunminjeongeum, which he personally created.
His illness worsened, he moved to the house of Grand Prince Yeongeung, and passed away.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Hunminjeongeum
keyAchievements.description: Created Hunminjeongeum, an efficient and scientific writing system that common people could easily learn.

keyAchievements.title: Support for scientific and technological development
keyAchievements.description: Fully supported the invention of the armillary sphere, Angbuilgu (sundial), Jagyeokru (water clock), and rain gauge, and sponsored scholars such as Jang Yeong-sil and Choe Hae-san.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening national defense
keyAchievements.description: Dispatched Yi Jong-mu to conquer Tsushima Island, and expanded the borders by sending Choe Yun-deok and Kim Jong-seo to the north to pioneer the Four Counties and Six Garrisons.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the political system
keyAchievements.description: Appointed Hwang Hui, Maeng Sa-seong, Yun Hoe, and Kim Jong-seo to preside over state affairs, which became the beginning of the State Council system.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the taxation system
keyAchievements.description: Established the land tax system of Joseon by enacting public laws such as the 6-grade land system and 9-grade harvest system.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of books
keyAchievements.description: Compiled books in various fields, including 'Songs of the Moon Shining on a Thousand Rivers', 'Songs of the Dragons Flying to Heaven', 'Straight Talk on Farming', and 'History of Goryeo'.

faq.question: When was King Sejong born?
faq.answer: Sejong was born on May 15, 1397 (April 10 in the lunar calendar) in Junsu-bang, Hanseong, as the third son of King Taejong and Queen Wongyeong.

faq.question: How did King Sejong ascend to the throne?
faq.answer: He was invested as Crown Prince when his older brother, Grand Prince Yangnyeong, was deposed, and ascended to the throne in 1418 following Taejong's abdication.

faq.question: What is King Sejong's greatest achievement?
faq.answer: One of his greatest achievements is the creation of Hunminjeongeum, an efficient and scientific writing system that the common people could easily learn.

faq.question: What achievements did King Sejong leave in the field of national defense?
faq.answer: He dispatched Yi Jong-mu to conquer Tsushima Island, and expanded the borders by sending Choe Yun-deok and Kim Jong-seo to the north to pioneer the Four Counties and Six Garrisons.

faq.question: When did King Sejong pass away?
faq.answer: Sejong passed away on March 30, 1450 (February 17 in the lunar calendar).

timeline.event: Temujin was born to his father Yesugei and mother Hoelun at Deligun Buldag near the Onon River.
Known as the birth year of Genghis Khan, but it varies among historical records (the current Mongolian holiday is November 14).
Visited Dei Sechen of the Onggirat tribe and formally married his daughter Borte.
Elected as the tribal chief of the old Khamag Mongol (Kiyat).
Various lords of the Kiyat Borjigin clan enthroned Temujin as the Khan of Khamag Mongol and bestowed the title Genghis Khan.
The power of the Kiyat tribe grew, and fought the Battle of Dalan Balzhut (Battle of the Thirteen Wings) against Jamukha.
Toghrul Khan, who had been ousted due to internal strife in the Keraites, was restored with Temujin's support.
With the help of the Keraites, Temujin attacked and destroyed the Jurkin clan, unifying the Borjigin clan.
Conducted an expedition and conquered the Merkit tribe remaining in the north of the plateau.
Held a Kurultai at Temeen Keer and declared the conquest of the Naimans.
Genghis Khan allied with the Keraites and attacked the Naimans in the Altai Mountains area.
The remnants of the Tatars, Merkits, and Tayichiud tribes allied and enthroned Jamukha as Khan. Annihilated Jamukha's alliance at the Battle of Koyiten against the northeastern alliance.
Temujin engaged the Tatars, killed their leader Jalin Bukha, and executed over a hundred remaining Tatars.
Ong Khan, deceived by Senggum's plot, attacked Temujin's pasture area, and Temujin successfully ambushed the main Keraite force, winning a great victory.
The Khamag Mongol army drove out and conquered the Naimans.
Temujin defeated the Western Naimans and Northern Merkits, the last major forces left on the Mongolian plateau, and executed Jamukha.
Temujin was enthroned as Genghis Khan at a meeting of tribal chiefs held by the Onon River, founding the Mongol Empire.
Sent his son Jochi to conquer and subjugate the forest tribes. Emperor Xiangzong of Western Xia promised subjugation to Mongolia and sent his daughter to marry Genghis Khan.
Signed a peace treaty with Western Xia and gained recognition of Mongol suzerainty. Subjugated the Kingdom of Qocho (Uyghurs of the Tian Shan).
Sent Kublai to conquer the Turkic nomadic Karluks, and their leader Arslan Khan surrendered. Genghis Khan notified the Jin Dynasty that he would cease tributary relations.
Declared and started war with the Jin Dynasty, defeating a 400,000-strong Jin army at the Battle of Yehuling.
Led his army to attack the Jin Dynasty, conquering and annexing Jin territory south of the Great Wall.
When the Jin Dynasty sued for peace, he took Princess Qiguo as a wife, received compensation, and retreated.
The Mongol army besieged and captured Zhongdu (Beijing), the former capital of the Jin Dynasty.
Genghis Khan dispatched a trade envoy to the Khwarazmian Empire, but Inalchuq, the governor of Otrar, killed the envoy.
Genghis Khan launched an invasion of Khwarazmia with a force of 200,000.
Genghis Khan finished his war of revenge against the Khwarazm-Shah and withdrew.
Hearing news of a rebellion in Western Xia, Genghis Khan left Peshawar and returned to the Mongolian plateau.
Genghis Khan arrived at the Mongolian plateau.
The Mongol army invaded Western Xia, attacking its borders.
Genghis Khan stayed at his main camp in the Liupan Mountains and accepted Western Xia's surrender, but rejected the Jin Dynasty's peace offer.
Genghis Khan died.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of the Mongol Empire and First Khagan
keyAchievements.description: At the 1206 Kurultai, he was enthroned as Genghis Khan, founding the Mongol Empire and becoming its first Khagan.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Mongol tribes
keyAchievements.description: As a military and political leader who unified various Mongol tribes, he conquered the tribes of the Mongolian steppes.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of vast territories
keyAchievements.description: Conquered vast territories ranging from the Korean Peninsula to Central Asia and Eastern Europe, including Mongolia and China.

keyAchievements.title: Merit-based military organization
keyAchievements.description: Led a strong army based on meritocracy and is evaluated as one of history's greatest conquerors and most successful military leaders.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Mongolian script
keyAchievements.description: With the help of a minister from the Naiman tribe, he helped establish the Mongolian language into a written script.

faq.question: What is Genghis Khan's real name?
faq.answer: Genghis Khan's real name is Borjigin Temujin.

faq.question: When did Genghis Khan ascend to the throne of the Mongol Empire?
faq.answer: At the meeting of tribal chiefs held by the Onon River in February 1206, Temujin was enthroned as Genghis Khan, founding the Mongol Empire and ascending as the first Khagan.

faq.question: How is Genghis Khan evaluated?
faq.answer: In Mongolia, he is praised as a god or national founding hero, but in Eastern Europe and Western Asia, which were invaded, there are contrasting evaluations of him as a mass murderer, looter, and destroyer of civilization.

faq.question: How did Genghis Khan's father die?
faq.answer: When Genghis Khan was 9 years old, his father Yesugei was poisoned by the Tatars, who had a long-standing feud with the Mongolian royal Borjigin clan.

faq.question: Why did Genghis Khan attack the Khwarazmian Empire?
faq.answer: Because Inalchuq, the governor of the city of Otrar, massacred an Islamic caravan of 450 people under Genghis Khan's protection, and the Shah of the Khwarazmian Empire refused to compensate for this.

timeline.event: Born in Pella, Macedonia, to King Philip II and Queen Olympias.
Tutored by the philosopher Aristotle.
Ascended to the throne of Macedonia following the assassination of his father.
Began his campaign against the Persian Empire, winning the Battle of the Granicus.
Defeated the Persian King Darius III at the Battle of Issus.
Founded the city of Alexandria in Egypt.
Decisively defeated Darius III at the Battle of Gaugamela, effectively ending the Persian Empire.
Invaded India and won the Battle of the Hydaspes, but his exhausted army mutinied and forced him to turn back.
Died in Babylon at the age of 32 from a mysterious illness.
Died at the age of 33 in Babylon.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: He completely dismantled the vast Achaemenid Persian Empire, the superpower of the day, in a rapid series of brilliant military campaigns.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a Vast Empire
keyAchievements.description: By the age of 30, he had created one of the largest empires in ancient history, stretching from Greece to northwestern India.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Hellenism
keyAchievements.description: His conquests facilitated the spread of Greek culture, language, and ideas across the Middle East and Asia, initiating the Hellenistic Age.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the formation of Hellenistic culture
keyAchievements.description: Founded cities named 'Alexandria' in various conquered regions, contributing to the fusion of Eastern and Western cultures and playing a major role in the formation of Hellenistic culture.

keyAchievements.title: East-West fusion policy
keyAchievements.description: Pursued East-West fusion policies by adopting Persian culture, marrying Greeks with residents of conquered regions, and employing Persian officials.

faq.question: Did Alexander really never lose a battle?
faq.answer: Yes, according to historical records, he never lost a single battle, despite often being heavily outnumbered, earning him a reputation as one of the greatest military commanders in history.

faq.question: How did he die?
faq.answer: The exact cause of his death at age 32 is unknown. Theories range from diseases like malaria or typhoid fever to poisoning or complications from his numerous battle wounds and heavy drinking.

faq.question: What happened to his empire after he died?
faq.answer: He died without naming a clear heir. His generals (the Diadochi) immediately fought over his empire, eventually fracturing it into several Hellenistic kingdoms, such as the Ptolemaic Kingdom in Egypt and the Seleucid Empire in Asia.

faq.question: How did Alexander the Great destroy the Persian Empire?
faq.answer: He crushed the Persian army in decisive battles such as the Battle of Issus and the Battle of Gaugamela, drove Darius III to his death, and destroyed the First Persian Empire.

faq.question: What cities did Alexander the Great build?
faq.answer: He built cities named 'Alexandria' in various conquered regions, the most famous being Alexandria in Egypt.

timeline.event: Walter Elias "Walt" Disney was born.
Decided to become a cartoonist and went to Kansas.
Founded the Disney Brothers Studio with his older brother Roy Disney.
Produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>.
Established a huge children's amusement park called Disneyland in the suburbs of Los Angeles.
Diagnosed with terminal lung cancer and died.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of the Walt Disney Studio
keyAchievements.description: Founded the Walt Disney Studio with his older brother Roy Disney in 1923.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Oswald the Lucky Rabbit character
keyAchievements.description: Developed a rabbit character, 'Oswald the Lucky Rabbit', amidst the prevailing cat characters of the time, introducing it as the first work of personality animation.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of Mickey Mouse
keyAchievements.description: Created the 'Mickey Mouse' character, which gained immense popularity, and named it 'Mickey' based on his wife Lillian Disney's suggestion.

keyAchievements.title: Production of the world's first sound animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced the third Mickey Mouse work, 'Steamboat Willie', which became the world's first sound animation and the official debut of Mickey Mouse.

keyAchievements.title: First Academy Award for a color animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced 'Flowers and Trees', the first color animation utilizing the three-color printing process, and enjoyed the honor of winning the first Academy Award for Best Short Subject, Cartoons.

keyAchievements.title: Production of the world's first feature-length animation
keyAchievements.description: Produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film, <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>, in 1937, creating massive buzz at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Disneyland
keyAchievements.description: Established a huge children's amusement park called Disneyland in the suburbs of Los Angeles in 1955, solidifying his position as a businessman beyond a film producer.

faq.question: When was Walt Disney born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Walt Disney was born on December 5, 1901, and died of terminal lung cancer on December 15, 1966.

faq.question: When was the Walt Disney Studio founded?
faq.answer: The Walt Disney Studio was founded in 1923 with his older brother Roy Disney.

faq.question: What is the representative character created by Walt Disney?
faq.answer: Walt Disney created 'Mickey Mouse', boosting its popularity, and this character represents The Walt Disney Company to this day.

faq.question: What was the world's first feature-length animation produced by Walt Disney?
faq.answer: He produced and released the world's first feature-length animated film, <Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs>, in 1937, achieving great success.

faq.question: Is the rumor that Walt Disney was cryogenically frozen true?
faq.answer: The rumor that Walt Disney was cryogenically frozen circulated for a time, but it is a false rumor; his remains were cremated and interred at Forest Lawn Memorial Park in Glendale.

timeline.event: Born in Milan, Ohio, and grew up in Port Huron, Michigan.
Developed an automatic telegraph.
Established the Menlo Park Laboratory, known as the world's first private research facility.
Developed the phonograph.
Developed the telephone transmitter.
Developed a new type of generator and lighting accessories.
Experimented with electric streetcars.
Built power plants and improved the industry.
Developed engine models and storage batteries.
Delivered a speech at the 50th-anniversary celebration of the invention of the incandescent light bulb at the age of 82.
Passed away at the age of 84 on October 18.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the phonograph
keyAchievements.description: One of the most famous inventions in the world, it is one of the numerous patents registered under Edison's name.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the light bulb
keyAchievements.description: An invention that illuminated city nights, brought development in the power generation and transmission/distribution sectors, and served as the catalyst for electricity to be supplied to every household.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Menlo Park Laboratory
keyAchievements.description: Established the Menlo Park Laboratory, known as the world's first private research facility, in 1876 and continued inventing.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of General Electric
keyAchievements.description: Later founded General Electric, based on the electric lighting company Edison had established.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Edison effect
keyAchievements.description: An important scientific discovery that formed the basis for the invention of the triode vacuum tube, opening the way for the invention of the radio.

faq.question: How many patents did Thomas Edison register?
faq.answer: There are 1,093 US patents registered under Edison's name, and it is unofficially said that he invented 2,332 items.

faq.question: How much formal education did Thomas Edison receive?
faq.answer: Edison received only 3 months of formal education, but thanks to the dedicated education of his mother, who was a teacher, he exhibited his talents.

faq.question: What kind of person was Thomas Edison to his family?
faq.answer: Edison devoted himself to inventions and business but neglected his family; he did not attend his first wife's funeral, and his children's lives were not good.

faq.question: How did Thomas Edison's inventions affect American science and technology?
faq.answer: Without academic theories, Edison's inventions through experimentation and curiosity made the United States a country that could overwhelm Europe in applied technology.

timeline.event: Born as Gaius Julius Caesar.
Became the head of the family at the age of 16 after his father's death.
Nominated as Flamen Dialis, the high priest of Jupiter, and married Cornelia.
Returned to Rome after Sulla's death and began working as a lawyer.
Studied abroad in Rhodes to perfect his rhetoric.
Elected as quaestor, and his aunt Julia and wife Cornelia died in the same year.
Elected as aedile and married Sulla's granddaughter Pompeia.
Ran for and was elected to the position of Pontifex Maximus, the head of the Roman state religion.
Divorced Pompeia due to the Bona Dea festival scandal while serving as praetor and was appointed governor of the province of Hispania Ulterior.
Elected as consul along with Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus and formed the First Triumvirate.
Appointed governor of the province of Gallia Cisalpina and Illyricum, beginning the Gallic Wars.
The figures of the Triumvirate met at Lucca in Gallia Cisalpina and extended Caesar's term as governor for 5 years.
Conducted the first Roman invasion of Britannia after resolving the Germanic tribe issues.
His daughter Julia died in childbirth, and Crassus was killed in the Battle of Carrhae, breaking the Triumvirate.
Essentially conquered Gaul by suppressing the massive rebellion led by Vercingetorix in central Gaul at the Battle of Alesia.
Crossed the Rubicon with just one legion, igniting the fuse of the civil war.
Pacified Hispania and had the praetor Lepidus appoint him as dictator.
Became consul for the second time and resigned from the dictatorship 11 days later.
Headed to Greece to confront Pompey.
Started the battle against Pompey's army at the plains of Pharsalus.
Assassinated by a group of senators led by Marcus Junius Brutus.
The Senate officially consecrated Caesar as a Roman god.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Gaul
keyAchievements.description: Conquered Gaul from 58 to 52 BC, expanding the territory of the Roman Empire to the North Sea.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the First Triumvirate
keyAchievements.description: Formed the First Triumvirate with Marcus Licinius Crassus and Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, dominating Roman politics for years.

keyAchievements.title: Reform of the Roman Republic
keyAchievements.description: After seizing power, implemented extensive reforms to Roman society and politics and highly centralized the Republic's aristocratic politics.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Dictator for Life
keyAchievements.description: Eventually declared himself Dictator for Life, playing a crucial role in the transformation of the Roman Republic into the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Invasion of Britannia
keyAchievements.description: In 55 BC, became the first Roman to carry out an invasion of Britannia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Acta Diurna
keyAchievements.description: Posted the Acta Diurna, equivalent to a modern newspaper, in the forum to disclose the minutes of the Senate and popular assemblies to the public.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the Agrarian Law
keyAchievements.description: Attempted social reform by proposing the Agrarian Law (lex Iulia de agraria), which redistributed public lands to the poor.

keyAchievements.title: Authorship of war records
keyAchievements.description: Personally authored war records such as the Gallic Wars and the Civil War to make his life known to future generations.

faq.question: What is the origin of Julius Caesar's name 'Caesar'?
faq.answer: Pliny the Elder claimed that an ancestor of Caesar was born via Caesarean section, hence the name meaning 'cut from the womb'. Other theories suggest it was due to thick hair, bright grey eyes, or an ancestor killing an elephant.

faq.question: Is it true that Julius Caesar was born via Caesarean section?
faq.answer: No. People had the name 'Caesar' before Julius Caesar, and at the time, Caesarean sections were only performed on dead women, whereas Caesar's mother Aurelia lived a long life after giving birth to him.

faq.question: What political line did Caesar follow?
faq.answer: Caesar aligned with the Populares and was a politician who preferred appealing directly to the voters instead of traditional processes.

faq.question: When did Caesar say his famous quote 'The die is cast'?
faq.answer: On January 12, 49 BC, when Caesar crossed the Rubicon with just one legion to start the civil war, Plutarch quoted him saying in Greek 'Let the die be cast!', while Suetonius wrote it in Latin as 'The die is cast'.

faq.question: How was Julius Caesar assassinated?
faq.answer: On March 15, 44 BC, he was assassinated by a group of senators led by Marcus Junius Brutus, who conspired to restore the Republic.

timeline.event: Helen Adams Keller was born in a house at Ivy Green in Tuscumbia, Alabama, USA.
Helen's mother, Kate Keller, was inspired by reading Charles Dickens' 'American Notes' and went to Baltimore to seek advice from J. Julian Chisolm.
Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house and began to teach her.
Helen enrolled in the Perkins School for the Blind.
Wrote 'The Frost King' at the age of 11.
Helen Keller and Anne Sullivan moved to New York to attend the Wright-Humason School for the Deaf and the Horace Mann School for the Deaf.
Returned to Massachusetts and attended The Cambridge School for Young Ladies.
Admitted to Radcliffe College, the women's college affiliated with Harvard University.
Published her autobiography 'The Story of My Life' with Anne Sullivan and Sullivan's husband, John Macy.
Graduated from Radcliffe College at the age of 24, becoming the first deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.
Wrote 'The World I Live In'.
Joined the Socialist Party of America at the age of 29.
Joined the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Published 'Out of the Dark', a series of essays on socialism.
Founded an organization called 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler.
Wrote articles for the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).
Appeared in the silent film 'Deliverance'.
Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU).
Published her spiritual autobiography 'My Religion'.
Anne Sullivan died.
Asked about the loyal dog Hachiko during a visit to Akita Prefecture, Japan, and received an Akita dog named 'Kamikaze-go'.
When 'Kamikaze-go' died, she received his older brother 'Kenzan-go' as an official gift from the Japanese government.
William Gibson's script for Playhouse 90 was realized as the first production of 'The Miracle Worker'.
Polly Thomson died of a stroke.
Suffered a series of strokes and spent her final years at home.
The film 'The Miracle Worker' starring Anne Bancroft and Patty Duke won Academy Awards.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from US President Lyndon B. Johnson.
Inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame at the New York World's Fair.
Died at the age of 88 at her home, Arcan Ridge, in Easton, Connecticut.
Helen Keller was selected as one of the 18 'most widely admired people of the 20th century' by Gallup.
The state of Alabama featured Helen Keller on its state quarter (25-cent coin).
A bronze statue of Helen Keller was added to the National Statuary Hall Collection.

keyAchievements.title: First deafblind person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree
keyAchievements.description: Graduated from Radcliffe College in 1904, becoming the first blind and deaf person to earn a Bachelor of Arts degree.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific author and educator
keyAchievements.description: Published a total of 12 books and numerous articles, and was active as an American author and educator.

keyAchievements.title: Socialist and human rights activist
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in various social movements including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Helen Keller International
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded 'Helen Keller International' with George Kessler in 1915, dedicated to research in vision, health, and nutrition.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to the founding of the ACLU
keyAchievements.description: Helped found the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) in 1920.

keyAchievements.title: National honors
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1964, inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame in 1965, and honored in various ways posthumously.

faq.question: When was Helen Keller born and when did she die?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was born on June 27, 1880, and died on June 1, 1968, at the age of 88.

faq.question: How did Helen Keller lose her sight and hearing?
faq.answer: At 19 months of age, she was diagnosed with scarlet fever and meningitis, which left her with lifelong visual and hearing impairments.

faq.question: Who had the greatest influence on Helen Keller's education?
faq.answer: Teacher Anne Sullivan arrived at Helen's house in March 1887 and had a decisive influence on her life by teaching her language by spelling words into her hand.

faq.question: What social movements did Helen Keller participate in?
faq.answer: Helen Keller was a socialist intellectual who practiced various human rights movements, including women's suffrage, pacifism, abolition of the death penalty, and opposition to child labor and racism.

faq.question: What is the famous work about Helen Keller's life?
faq.answer: The film 'The Miracle Worker', which depicts Helen Keller's childhood and her relationship with Anne Sullivan, is the most well-known, and the 1962 film won Academy Awards.

timeline.event: Born to his father Shu Lianghe and mother Yan Zhengzai in Zou, Changping, Qufu in the State of Lu.
His father, Shu Lianghe, died.
Married.
His mother, Yan Zhengzai, died.
Married the daughter of the Bingguan family of the State of Song.
Had a son, Li (鯉).
Opened an academy in the State of Lu and began teaching students.
Started his career as an official, passing through positions such as Weili (accountant) and Sizhi (manager of pastures).
Fled to the State of Qi following Duke Zhao of Lu due to the 'Rebellion of the Three Huan' in the State of Lu.
Returned from the State of Qi.
Became Zhongduzai (magistrate of Zhongdu).
Rose to the position of Da Sikou (Minister of Crime).
Earned the enmity of Ji Sunsi, left the State of Lu, and wandered through various states for 14 years.
Suffered grief as he successively lost his son Boyu and his disciples Yan Hui and Zilu.
Returned to his hometown of Qufu and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars.
Passed away with his disciples watching over him.
Emperor Xuanzong of Tang posthumously honored Confucius as 'King Wenxuan'.
Emperor Zhenzong of Song posthumously titled him 'Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Most Sage King Wenxuan).
Came to be called 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Wang' (The Great, Supreme Sage and King Wenxuan).

timeline.year: After the founding of the Ming Dynasty
timeline.event: Awarded the title 'Zhisheng Xianshi' (The Most Sage and Ancient Teacher).
Awarded the title 'Dacheng Zhisheng Wenxuan Xianshi' (The Great, Supreme Sage and Ancient Teacher Wenxuan).

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of China's first school
keyAchievements.description: Established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and taught students.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the evil practice of human sacrifice
keyAchievements.description: Advised the king and abolished the evil practice of human sacrifice (sati) which had continued until then.

keyAchievements.title: Recovery of the lost territory of the State of Lu
keyAchievements.description: Recovered the old territory that the State of Lu had lost through brilliant strategies in negotiations with the State of Qi.

keyAchievements.title: Education on the Six Classics
keyAchievements.description: Educated his disciples using the Six Classics as textbooks: the 'Book of Changes', 'Book of Odes', 'Book of Documents', 'Book of Rites', 'Classic of Music', and 'Spring and Autumn Annals'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of the philosophy of Ren (Benevolence)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated humanism, placing 'Ren' (Benevolence) as the ultimate orientation of human behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Cultivating younger scholars
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, he returned to his hometown and devoted himself to cultivating younger scholars, producing many outstanding disciples.

faq.question: Who was Confucius?
faq.answer: He was a politician, philosopher, and educator in the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China, and is known as the founder of Confucianism.

faq.question: What is the core philosophy of Confucius?
faq.answer: Contained in the 'Analects', a collection of his words and deeds, he advocated humanism, placing the ultimate orientation of human behavior on 'Ren' (Benevolence).

faq.question: What educational activities did Confucius engage in?
faq.answer: He established an academy for the first time in Chinese history and strove to cultivate younger scholars by teaching classics such as the Book of Odes, the Book of Documents, and the Book of Changes.

faq.question: What was Confucius's political ideal?
faq.answer: He advocated rule by virtue, governing the people and the country with virtue rather than law, and humanism, which places the highest value on people.

faq.question: When did Confucius die?
faq.answer: He passed away in 479 BC at the age of 72, with his disciples watching over him.

timeline.event: Birth of Nikola Tesla.
Enrolled at the Technische Universität Graz in Austria.
Obtained a job at the Paris branch of the Edison Telephone Company through the arrangement of the Puskás brothers.
Left for the United States with a letter of recommendation from Charles Batchelor and met Thomas Edison.
Met A. K. Brown, a manager at the Western Union Telegraph Company, and founded a company bearing Tesla's name.
The Tesla Electric Company was newly founded.
Westinghouse built a power plant using Tesla's alternating current (AC) system to supply electricity to a mining area.
Lectured at the electrical engineering congress and presented a mechanical oscillator and electrical oscillator.
Demonstrated wireless communication for the first time during speeches at the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia and the National Electric Light Association in St. Louis.
The Niagara Falls Commission signed a contract with Westinghouse, bringing the War of the Currents to an end.
First realized wireless communication via radio.
A major fire occurred at his laboratory at 33-35 South Fifth Avenue.
Reported the dangers of X-rays to the New York Academy.
Applied for a wireless communication patent.
Left New York and demonstrated a remote-controlled boat in Chicago.
Accepted Leonard Curtis's offer and arrived in Colorado Springs.
Measured lightning during storms, discovering its regular periodicity and realizing it was a standing wave.
Completed his work in Colorado and prepared to leave.
His wireless communication patent was approved.
The New York Times published an article stating that Edison and Tesla had been chosen as joint recipients of the Nobel Prize in Physics.
Reuters reported that the Nobel Committee had selected Professors William Henry Bragg and William Lawrence Bragg as the laureates.
Originated the basic concept of radar for military purposes.
Death of Nikola Tesla.
The Conférence Générale des Poids et Mesures in Paris established the 'tesla' as the SI unit of magnetic flux density.
A statue commemorating the 150th anniversary of Nikola Tesla's birth was officially unveiled in Queen Victoria Park in Niagara Falls, Ontario, Canada.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Alternating Current (AC) System
keyAchievements.description: Invented transformers and the alternating current system, making significant contributions to commercial electricity.

keyAchievements.title: Advancement of Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Well known as the figure who enabled revolutionary advancements in electromagnetism in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Forming the Foundation of the Modern AC System
keyAchievements.description: His patents and theoretical work formed the foundation of the modern AC system, including the polyphase power distribution system and the AC motor.

keyAchievements.title: Realization of Wireless Communication
keyAchievements.description: Called the father of wireless communication for first realizing wireless communication via radio in 1894.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Rotating Magnetic Field Principle
keyAchievements.description: Developed a completely new system utilizing the principle of the rotating magnetic field based on an idea that came to him while walking in a park with a friend.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Polyphase System
keyAchievements.description: Developed the polyphase system, which includes the polyphase induction motor, split-phase induction motor, and polyphase synchronous motor, enabling the generation, transmission, distribution, and mechanical utilization of electricity.

keyAchievements.title: X-ray Research
keyAchievements.description: Took X-ray photographs prior to Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen's discovery, realized the harmful effects of X-rays on the human body, and reported the dangers to the New York Academy in 1897.

keyAchievements.title: Ideas on Wireless Energy Transmission and Communication
keyAchievements.description: Conceived ideas for controlling the world's weather using magnetic fields, and methods for transmitting energy and communicating wirelessly.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Fluidic Diode
keyAchievements.description: While developing a large turbine at the Waterside Power Station, he developed a special valved conduit that allowed the use of combustible fuel, also known as the 'fluidic diode'.

keyAchievements.title: Origination of the Basic Concept of Radar
keyAchievements.description: Originated the basic concept of radar for military purposes to track German submarines during World War I around 1917.

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the Theory of Electrogravitics
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a new theory of gravity called electrogravitics, refuting Einstein's theory by arguing that the curving of space is impossible and the universe is filled with ether.

faq.question: Who was Nikola Tesla?
faq.answer: Nikola Tesla was a Serbian-American inventor, physicist, and electrical engineer who spearheaded the Second Industrial Revolution by inventing the transformer and AC system and laying the foundation for the modern AC system.

faq.question: What are Tesla's major achievements?
faq.answer: He first realized the AC system, polyphase induction motor, and wireless communication, and made illustrious contributions to the field of electromagnetism, including X-ray research, ideas on wireless energy transmission, and originating the basic concept of radar.

faq.question: What was Tesla's relationship with Edison?
faq.answer: Tesla worked at Edison's company and improved the performance of generators, but left the company after not receiving a promised bonus. He subsequently engaged in the 'War of the Currents' with his AC system against Edison's DC system.

faq.question: Did Tesla win a Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: There was an article in 1915 mentioning him as a joint recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics, but he was never officially notified, and the final laureates were other individuals.

faq.question: Was Tesla's method of invention unique?
faq.answer: He had his own world of imagination in his mind, and he could mentally resolve the entire process required for an invention without any special blueprints or physical experiments.

faq.question: What are some examples of Tesla's name being used?
faq.answer: His name is used for the SI unit of magnetic flux density 'tesla (T)', a crater on the far side of the Moon, the asteroid '2244 Tesla', a power plant and airport in Serbia, and the electric car company 'Tesla Motors'.

faq.question: What kind of personality did Tesla have?
faq.answer: He had a peculiar personality from a young age, enjoying writing poetry and inventing, having a habit of measuring the volume of food during meals, and showing a fascination with sparkling crystals. He also suffered from pathological phobias and compulsions.

timeline.event: Born in Zundert, Netherlands.
His younger brother, Theodorus 'Theo', was born.
Was sent to the village school in Zundert.
Enrolled in a boarding school in Zevenbergen.
Was sent to a middle school in Tilburg.
Dropped out of school and returned home.
Found employment at the Goupil & Cie gallery in The Hague.
Transferred to the Goupil gallery's London branch on Southampton Street.
Was arranged a transfer to Paris.
Was dismissed from the Goupil gallery; worked as an unpaid supply teacher at a boarding school in Ramsgate, England; lived as an assistant to a Methodist minister.
Prepared for the theology entrance exam with his uncle, Johannes Stricker, in Amsterdam.
Failed the theology entrance exam and left his uncle's house; took a 3-month course at a Protestant missionary school in Laeken near Brussels.
Took up a post as a lay missionary in the mining village of Petit Wasmes in the Borinage, Belgium; was dismissed by the missionary organization.
Returned to Cuesmes and stayed with a miner; took up art in earnest at Theo's recommendation.
Left for Brussels and enrolled at the Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts.
Returned to Etten for a long stay with his family; proposed to his uncle's daughter, Cornelia 'Kee' Vos-Stricker, but was rejected.

Learned oil painting from Anton Mauve and set up a studio; began a relationship with Clasina Maria 'Sien' Hoornik, an alcoholic prostitute; spent three weeks in the hospital with an attack of gonorrhea.
Left Sien and her children; moved to Drenthe province in the northern Netherlands; returned to his parents' home in Nuenen.
Fell in love with his neighbor Margot Begemann, but she attempted suicide by poison due to their families' opposition to marriage.
His father died on March 26; completed 'The Potato Eaters'; 'The Potato Eaters' was first publicly exhibited in The Hague.
Moved to Antwerp.

Took the entrance exam for the higher level of the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in Antwerp and was admitted; was hospitalized due to overwork, poor diet, and excessive smoking; moved to Paris, lived in his brother Theo's apartment, and studied art at Fernand Cormon's studio.
Moved to Asnières-sur-Seine, a northwestern suburb of Paris; befriended Paul Gauguin; held an exhibition in Montmartre with Bernard, Anquetin, and Toulouse-Lautrec.

Left Paris for rest and moved to Arles in southern France; rented four rooms at 2 Place Lamartine in Arles; Paul Gauguin arrived in Arles; following an argument with Gauguin, he cut off his left ear and was hospitalized.

Returned to the Yellow House in Arles; a petition by townspeople led the police to close his house, sending him back to the hospital; voluntarily admitted himself to the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence.

Albert Aurier praised his work in the 'Mercure de France'; received an invitation from Les XX in Brussels to participate in their annual exhibition; participated in the 6th Salon des Indépendants on the Champs-Élysées in Paris; left the clinic in Saint-Rémy and moved to Auvers-sur-Oise, a suburb of Paris.
Attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver on July 27.
Died on July 29.
Was buried in the municipal cemetery of Auvers-sur-Oise on July 30.
His brother Theo died on January 25.
Theo's widow, Johanna van Gogh-Bonger, had Theo's body exhumed and reburied next to Vincent's grave.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: One of the most famous and influential Post-Impressionist painters in the history of Western art, contributing to the revival of Expressionism in modern art.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Artist
keyAchievements.description: Created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

keyAchievements.title: Bold Colors and Brushwork
keyAchievements.description: Established a unique style characterized by bold colors and dramatic brushwork in landscapes, still lifes, portraits, and self-portraits.

keyAchievements.title: Symbolic Use of Color
keyAchievements.description: Believed that color carried psychological and moral weight beyond simple depiction, especially using yellow as a symbol of sunlight, life, and the divine.

keyAchievements.title: Self-Portrait Series
keyAchievements.description: Produced over 43 self-portraits between 1885 and 1889, expressing a high level of introspection and various mental and physical states.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Global Fame
keyAchievements.description: Though he sold only one painting during his lifetime, after his death, his art and life story captured the public imagination as a symbol of the misunderstood genius, and his works are among the most expensive paintings ever sold.

faq.question: How many paintings did Vincent van Gogh sell during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, Van Gogh sold only one painting: 'The Red Vineyard at Arles'.

faq.question: How many artworks did Vincent van Gogh create in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, in just over a decade.

faq.question: Why did Vincent van Gogh cut off his ear?
faq.answer: Following an argument with Paul Gauguin, he seemingly hallucinated and severed all or part of his left ear with a razor, indicating that he suffered an acute mental breakdown.

faq.question: How did Vincent van Gogh die?
faq.answer: On July 27, 1890, at the age of 37, he attempted suicide by shooting himself in the chest with a revolver in a wheat field, and died of an infection from the wound two days later on July 29.

faq.question: What was the relationship like between Vincent van Gogh and his brother Theo?
faq.answer: His younger brother Theo supported Vincent financially and emotionally, and the two maintained a deep friendship, exchanging hundreds of letters throughout their lives.

faq.question: Which of Van Gogh's paintings sold for the highest price?
faq.answer: On May 15, 1990, his 'Portrait of Dr. Gachet' (first version) sold for $82.5 million at Christie's, setting a new record high price.

timeline.event: Birth of Gwanggaeto the Great, the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.
Accession of his father, King Gogugyang, following the death of his uncle, King Sosurim.
Invested as Crown Prince.
Ascended to the throne as the 19th monarch of Goguryeo following King Gogugyang, and began using the era name Yeongnak.
Led 40,000 troops to attack the northern border of Baekje, capturing over 10 fortresses including Seokhyeonseong.
When Baekje attacked Goguryeo's southern border, ordered his generals to repel them.
Conquered the Khitan, recovering over 10,000 people who had been taken captive in the 8th year of King Sosurim (378).
Captured Gwanmiseong, a northern stronghold of Baekje, after a 20-day siege.
Death of King Jinsa of Baekje and accession of King Asin.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Gwanmiseong. Founded 9 Buddhist temples in Pyongyang.
Repelled a Baekje attack at Sugokseong. Built 7 fortresses on the southern border to defend against Baekje invasions.
Captured over 8,000 Baekje troops alive and achieved a great victory at Paesu. Advanced to Yeomsu (Salt Water), conquered and subjugated the Khitan tribe Paeryeo.

Launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and laid siege to Wiryeseong. Received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje and returned triumphantly, taking the king's brother and 10 ministers as hostages.
Conquered the Sushen to stabilize the northeastern border region.
Conducted a royal tour to Pyongyang. When Baekje and Wa (Japan) attacked Silla, Silla sent envoys to Pyongyang to plead for help.

Dispatched a large army of 50,000 to Silla to defeat the Wa forces. Pursued and repelled the Wa forces as far as Jongbalseong in Geumgwan Gaya. Replaced the king of Silla from Naemul Maripgan to Silseong Maripgan. King Murong Sheng of Later Yan attacked Sinseong and Namsosŏng.
Attacked and captured Sukgunseong of Later Yan.
Annihilated Wa forces that invaded the Daifang region. Invaded Later Yan and attacked as far as Yan Commandery, capturing it.
Defeated the Later Yan army at Yodongseong (Liaodong Fortress).
Defeated the Later Yan army at Mokjeoseong, firmly securing the occupation of Liaodong.
Mobilized 50,000 men to defeat the Later Yan army and captured massive spoils of war. Captured 6 fortresses of Later Yan on the way back.
When Go Un, who was of Goguryeo descent, founded Northern Yan, he established friendly relations, thereby stabilizing the western border.
Built 6 fortresses including Doksanseong in the east of the country and relocated households from Pyongyang to live there. Conducted a royal tour to the south.
Attacked and subjugated Eastern Buyeo.
Passed away.
King Jangsu erected a stele at his tomb and, in accordance with his will, appointed tomb keepers to manage the royal mausoleum.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Baekje
keyAchievements.description: Launched a massive attack on Baekje in 396, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and receiving the surrender of King Asin.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Khitan and Sushen
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Khitan in 392 to recover captured subjects, and conquered the Sushen in 398 to stabilize the northeastern border region.

keyAchievements.title: Rescue of Silla and Defeat of Wa
keyAchievements.description: Dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla in 400 to defeat the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and established influence over Silla by doing things such as replacing its king.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in War Against Later Yan
keyAchievements.description: Secured the occupation of Liaodong by winning fierce wars against Later Yan, and stabilized the western border through friendly relations with Northern Yan.

keyAchievements.title: Subjugation of Eastern Buyeo
keyAchievements.description: Strengthened Goguryeo's northern influence by attacking and subjugating Eastern Buyeo in 410.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening Internal Affairs
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of the state by emphasizing Pyongyang, building 9 temples and fortresses there, and establishing a system for maintaining previous royal tombs and tomb keepers.

faq.question: What was the name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His name was Damdeok or An.

faq.question: Which monarch of Goguryeo was Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: He was the 19th monarch of Goguryeo.

faq.question: When did Gwanggaeto the Great reign?
faq.answer: He reigned from the 5th lunar month of 391 to the 10th lunar month of 412.

faq.question: What was the era name of Gwanggaeto the Great?
faq.answer: His era name was Yeongnak.

faq.question: What results did Gwanggaeto the Great achieve in the war against Baekje?
faq.answer: In 396, he launched a massive attack on Baekje, capturing 58 fortresses and over 700 villages north of the Han River, and received the surrender of King Asin of Baekje.

faq.question: How did Gwanggaeto the Great rescue Silla?
faq.answer: In 400, he dispatched an army of 50,000 to Silla, defeated the allied forces of Baekje and Wa, and exerted influence over Silla by replacing its king.

faq.question: When was the Gwanggaeto Stele erected?
faq.answer: The Stele of Gwanggaeto the Great was erected by King Jangsu in 414.

timeline.event: Born in the city of Rome.
Death of his father, Octavius.
Delivered the funeral oration for his maternal grandmother, Julia Caesaris.
Donned the toga virilis and was elected to the College of Pontiffs.
Presided over the Greek games in honor of the Temple of Venus Genetrix, built by Caesar.
Assassination of his adoptive father, Julius Caesar.
Arrived in Rome and met Mark Antony.
Defeated two legions under Antony's command.
Admitted to the Senate and granted military command.
Defeated Antony's forces at the Battle of Forum Gallorum and the Battle of Mutina.
Appointed consul.
Formed the Second Triumvirate with Antony and Lepidus near Bologna.
The Roman Senate declared Julius Caesar a god.
Defeated the forces of Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi.
Ended the siege of Lucius Antonius at Perusia and received his surrender.
Executed 300 senators and equestrians who had collaborated with Lucius Antonius.
Signed the Treaty of Brundisium with Antony.
Married his older sister, Octavia Minor, to Antony.
Concluded a temporary peace agreement, the Pact of Misenum, with Sextus Pompey.
Divorced Scribonia and married Livia Drusilla.
Agreed to extend the Triumvirate for another five years.
Completely destroyed Sextus Pompey's fleet at the Battle of Naulochus.
The Senate stripped Antony of his consular powers and declared war on Egypt.
Defeated the fleets of Antony and Cleopatra at the Battle of Actium.
Began holding the consulship every year.
Achieved victory in Alexandria, leading to the suicides of Antony and Cleopatra.
Granted the title of 'First Citizen of the State' (princeps civitatis) by the Senate.
After ending the civil wars, declared the return of extraordinary powers to the Senate and was awarded the title 'Augustus'.
Married his daughter Julia to his sister's son, Marcellus.
Marcellus died, and Augustus resigned from the consulship.
Personally undertook the construction of roads.
Finally suppressed the rebellion in the Cantabria region of Spain.
Assumed the office of Pontifex Maximus after the death of Lepidus.
His stepson Drusus died after falling from a horse in Germania.
Elected consul, and his maternal grandson Gaius Caesar began his political career.
Elected consul, his maternal grandson Lucius Caesar began his political career, and he was granted the title of 'Father of the Country' (pater patriae) by the Senate and the Roman people.
His maternal grandson Lucius Caesar died young.
His maternal grandson Gaius Caesar died young, and he adopted Tiberius and Agrippa Postumus as his sons.
Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to fund pensions for active and retired soldiers.
Three Roman legions were annihilated by Germanic tribes in the Teutoburg Forest.
Tiberius was granted all the prerogatives held by Augustus.
Died in Nola, and the Senate and the popular assembly declared him a god.

keyAchievements.title: First Emperor of the Roman Empire
keyAchievements.description: Reigned as the first Emperor of the Roman Empire and the founder of the Julio-Claudian dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Pax Romana
keyAchievements.description: His rule ushered in an era of peace and prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), which sustained peace in the Mediterranean world for over two centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Imperial Territory and Border Protection
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the territory of the Roman Empire, protected its borders and allies, and concluded a peace treaty with Parthia.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the State System
keyAchievements.description: Reformed Rome's taxation system, built an overland transportation network, and established a standing army, a small navy, and the Praetorian Guard.

keyAchievements.title: Redevelopment of the City of Rome
keyAchievements.description: Established police and fire-fighting forces in Rome and extensively redeveloped large parts of the city.

keyAchievements.title: Recording the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'
keyAchievements.description: Before his death, he left behind a record of his accomplishments known as the 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti'.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Title 'Augustus'
keyAchievements.description: Received the title 'Augustus', meaning 'the revered one', from the Senate.

keyAchievements.title: Granted the Title 'First Citizen of the State'
keyAchievements.description: Was granted the title 'princeps civitatis' (First Citizen of the State) by the Senate, meaning the leading figure.

keyAchievements.title: Securing Military Command
keyAchievements.description: Became the supreme commander of the Roman military and held the authority to celebrate triumphs.

keyAchievements.title: Tax Reform
keyAchievements.description: Executed tax reforms, including a fixed-rate direct tax on provincials and the introduction of a tax collection system managed by civil servants.

keyAchievements.title: Naming of the Month 'August'
keyAchievements.description: The former 'sixth month' (Sextilis) was renamed 'Augustus' (August) in his honor.

keyAchievements.title: Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Contributed so significantly to the architectural development of Rome that he famously boasted, 'I found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Military Pension Fund
keyAchievements.description: Established the 'Military Treasury' (aerarium militare) to provide pensions for active duty and retired soldiers.

faq.question: What was the real name of Augustus?
faq.answer: Before being adopted by Caesar, his name was Gaius Octavius, and after adoption, he became known as Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus.

faq.question: How did Augustus become the first emperor of the Roman Empire?
faq.answer: After the collapse of the Second Triumvirate, he monopolized power and, upon receiving the title 'Augustus' from the Senate in 27 BC, became the first emperor of the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What was the greatest achievement of the Augustan era?
faq.answer: His rule brought about an era of prosperity known as the 'Pax Romana' (Roman Peace), sustaining peace in the Mediterranean world for more than two centuries.

faq.question: Did Augustus enjoy gambling?
faq.answer: Contrary to his stoic image, he enjoyed gambling, particularly dice and odd-even games, and often built friendships by intentionally losing money.

faq.question: Why was the month of August named after Augustus?
faq.answer: By decision of the Roman Senate, the month was named in his honor to commemorate his capture of Alexandria and ascent to power in August of 30 BC.

timeline.event: Born in Schönhausen, Prussia
Enrolled at the Plamannsche Lehranstalt in Berlin
Enrolled in law at the University of Göttingen
Began practical training as a legal clerk after graduating from university
Began military service
Started life as a farm manager after completing military service
Entered politics by being elected as a member of the United Diet of Saxony
Acted as a counter-revolutionary during the March Revolution in Berlin
Elected as a member of the Prussian United Diet
Appointed as the Prussian envoy to the Federal Diet in Frankfurt
Appointed as the Prussian ambassador to Russia
Appointed Minister President of Prussia after serving as ambassador to France
Delivered the 'Blood and Iron' speech in parliament
Victory in the Second Schleswig War against Denmark
Victory in the Austro-Prussian War and signing of the Peace of Prague
Formation of the North German Confederation
Victory in the Franco-Prussian War
Proclamation of the German Empire and appointment as its first Chancellor
Began the 'Kulturkampf' (culture struggle) suppressing Catholics in southern Germany
Establishment of the Reichsbank (Imperial Bank)
Presided over the Congress of Berlin following the Russo-Turkish War
Enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws'
Implemented health insurance
Implemented accident insurance and acquired colonies in Togo and Cameroon, Africa
Acquired the colony of German East Africa
Implemented old age and disability insurance
Resigned and retired from politics due to policy conflicts with Wilhelm II
Death

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Germany and Creation of the German Empire
keyAchievements.description: Achieved the unification of Germany and established the German Empire by expanding Prussia's military and winning wars against Denmark, Austria, and France.

keyAchievements.title: Blood and Iron Policy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the nickname 'Iron Chancellor' through a speech advocating military expansion upon taking office, using it as a basis to strengthen military power.

keyAchievements.title: Maintaining the Balance of Power in Europe
keyAchievements.description: As Chancellor of the German Empire, he dominated European diplomacy and strove to maintain a balance of power among the great powers by forging agreements such as the League of the Three Emperors and the Triple Alliance.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Social Security Systems
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for a welfare state by implementing the world's first social security systems, including health insurance, accident insurance, and old age and disability insurance.

keyAchievements.title: Acquisition of Colonies
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to securing German colonies in Africa, such as Togo, Cameroon, and German East Africa, by encouraging the development of German capitalism and colonial expansion.

faq.question: Why is Bismarck called the 'Iron Chancellor'?
faq.answer: He earned this nickname from his 'Blood and Iron' speech in 1862, which advocated military expansion, though he shifted to a pacifist policy after unification.

faq.question: How did Bismarck achieve the unification of Germany?
faq.answer: Following his 'Blood and Iron' policy, he expanded the military and won wars against Denmark, Austria, and France, leading to the proclamation of the German Empire and achieving unification.

faq.question: What were Bismarck's major domestic policies?
faq.answer: He implemented the 'Kulturkampf' to suppress Catholics, enacted the 'Anti-Socialist Laws' to suppress socialism, and introduced the world's first social security systems.

faq.question: Why did Bismarck resign from the chancellorship?
faq.answer: He resigned and retired from politics in 1890 due to policy conflicts with Emperor Wilhelm II.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Tsar Alexis Mikhailovich and his second wife, Natalya Kirillovna Naryshkina.

Ascended to the throne after the early death of his older half-brother Fyodor III, but lost power due to a coup by his older half-sister Princess Sophia, and spent his childhood and youth in the Foreign Quarter.
Married Eudoxia Lopukhina, daughter of a Russian aristocrat.
Taking advantage of the aristocracy's withdrawal of support for Sophia's regime, he staged a coup, exiled Sophia to a convent, and seized control of state affairs.
Participated in the resumed Azov campaigns against the Ottoman Empire as an artilleryman.
Organized a navy and attacked Azov again, easily capturing it.
Following the death of his half-brother Ivan V, he became the sole autocrat of the Tsardom of Russia.
Joined an alliance with Denmark and Poland against Charles XII of Sweden and entered the Great Northern War.
Began the construction of the new capital, St. Petersburg, on the barren coastal lands of the Baltic Sea acquired as a result of the Northern War.
Inflicted a decisive defeat on the Swedish army, personally commanded by Charles XII, at Poltava.
The Ottoman Empire declared war on the Tsardom of Russia after Charles XII formed an alliance with them.
When surrounded by Ottoman forces along the Pruth River, he surrendered to minimize casualties, giving up Azov and the Black Sea fleet in exchange.
Defeated the Swedish fleet at the Battle of Gangut.
His arch-rival Charles XII died in battle without an heir.
His only son, Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich, died in prison from the aftereffects of torture.
The Great Northern War concluded with a Russian victory, the Tsardom of Russia was proclaimed the Russian Empire, and Peter was given the title of Imperator.
While inspecting the construction site of St. Petersburg, he jumped into the water to save a drowning soldier and contracted pneumonia.
Died from complications of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Launch and Development of the Russian Empire
keyAchievements.description: He advanced the Tsardom of Russia through Westernization policies and territorial expansion, and launched the Russian Empire by adopting the title of Imperator.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of Westernization Policies
keyAchievements.description: To modernize Russia, which still bore strong remnants of the Mongol Empire, he promoted sweeping Westernization policies, including changes to women's clothing, a beard tax, and administrative reform.

keyAchievements.title: Creation and Strengthening of the Navy
keyAchievements.description: Realizing the need for a fleet during the Azov campaigns, he organized a powerful navy that contributed decisively to the capture of Azov and the victory in the Great Northern War.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Great Northern War
keyAchievements.description: He secured trade routes to the Baltic Sea by winning the Great Northern War against Sweden, transforming Russia into a major power on the Baltic coast.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of St. Petersburg
keyAchievements.description: Built the new capital, St. Petersburg, on the barren lands of the Baltic Sea acquired from the Northern War, creating a city that symbolized Russia's Westernization.

keyAchievements.title: Administrative and Academic Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Created permanent administrative bodies modeled after Prussia, established the Table of Ranks, simplified the Cyrillic alphabet, and encouraged scholarship by founding academic institutions.

faq.question: Why did Peter I promote Westernization policies?
faq.answer: He prioritized Westernization policies to modernize Russia, which lagged behind Western Europe in development and retained strong remnants of the Mongol Empire.

faq.question: How did Peter I come to create a navy?
faq.answer: Experiencing difficulties in besieging fortresses without a fleet during the Azov campaigns against the Ottoman Empire, he pursued the construction of a fleet using knowledge he had acquired since childhood.

faq.question: What happened to Peter I's son?
faq.answer: His only son, Tsarevich Alexei Petrovich, opposed his father's Westernization policies and attempted to orchestrate an assassination. Upon being discovered, he fled into exile but eventually returned, and died in prison from the aftereffects of torture.

faq.question: How did Peter I die?
faq.answer: In December 1724, while inspecting the construction site of St. Petersburg, he jumped into the water to save a drowning soldier. This caused him to contract pneumonia, leading to his death on February 8 of the following year.

timeline.event: Born in the vilayet of Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece) under the rule of the Ottoman Empire.
Enrolled in the Salonica Military School.
Enrolled in the Manastir Military High School.
Enrolled in the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military Academy.
Graduated from the Ottoman Military College.
Promoted to Senior Captain.
Assigned to the Third Army headquarters in Manastir.
Appointed as the Ottoman railway inspector of Eastern Rumelia.
Joined the Young Turk Revolution.
Returned to Istanbul after suppressing a tribal rebellion.
Contributed to the suppression of the 31 March Incident, a counter-coup in Istanbul.
Assigned to Ottoman Albania.
Participated in large-scale military maneuvers in Picardy, France, as an Ottoman observer officer.
Briefly served in the Ottoman Ministry of War in Istanbul.
Deployed to Ottoman Tripolitania (modern-day Libya) and fought in the Italo-Turkish War.
Achieved a major victory in the Battle of Tobruk.
Suffered vision impairment in his left eye due to an injury sustained at the Battle of Derna.
Appointed commander of the Ottoman forces at Derna.
Returned from the Italo-Turkish War front upon the outbreak of the Balkan Wars.
The Italo-Turkish War ended, and under the Treaty of Ouchy, Ottoman Tripolitania and other territories were ceded to the Kingdom of Italy.
Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Sakarya, putting an end to the Megali Idea (Greater Greece).
The date of the great victory against Greece, which is celebrated as a national holiday in Turkey.
Abolished the Sultanate and deposed Mehmed VI.
Signed a new treaty (the Treaty of Lausanne) with the Allied Powers.
Moved the capital from Istanbul to Ankara, proclaimed the republic, and became the first President.
Abolished the Caliphate, an institution that had existed for 1,300 years since 632.
Implemented dress reforms to emancipate women's attire, instituted co-educational equality, and replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.
Amended the civil code to ban polygamy and establish monogamy.
Abolished the Arabic script for the Turkish language and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.
Granted voting rights to women.
The Turkish parliament bestowed upon him the surname 'Atatürk', meaning 'Father of the Turks'.
Signs of Atatürk's deteriorating health began to appear.
Suffered from severe illness during a trip to Yalova and was diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver.
Died at the age of 57 at the Dolmabahçe Palace.

keyAchievements.title: Founder and First President of the Republic of Turkey
keyAchievements.description: As the founder and first President of the Republic of Turkey, he led the Turkish War of Independence.

keyAchievements.title: Victory in the Battle of Gallipoli
keyAchievements.description: An outstanding general who successfully halted the offensive of the British and French forces at the Battle of Gallipoli during World War I.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Sultanate and Caliphate
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Sultanate in 1922, deposing Mehmed VI, and abolished the Caliphate in 1924.

keyAchievements.title: Legislation of Secularism
keyAchievements.description: Enshrined secularism, the founding principle of the Republic of Turkey, into law.

keyAchievements.title: Dress Reforms and Ensuring Women's Right to Education
keyAchievements.description: Enacted dress reforms to emancipate women's attire and guaranteed the previously forbidden right of women to an education, instituting co-educational equality.

keyAchievements.title: Abolition of the Islamic Calendar and Introduction of the Gregorian Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Replaced the Islamic calendar with the European Gregorian calendar.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Civil Code and Establishment of Monogamy
keyAchievements.description: Amended the civil code to prohibit polygamy and establish monogamy.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Latin Alphabet
keyAchievements.description: Abolished the Arabic script used for writing Turkish and replaced it with a Latin-based alphabet.

keyAchievements.title: Granting Women the Right to Vote
keyAchievements.description: Granted women the right to vote in 1930.

faq.question: What was Atatürk's original name?
faq.answer: His childhood name was Mustafa. The name 'Kemal' was given to him by his mathematics teacher, who praised his mathematical abilities and mature demeanor.

faq.question: What does the title 'Atatürk' mean and when was it bestowed?
faq.answer: The title 'Atatürk' means 'Father of the Turks' and was bestowed upon him by the Turkish parliament in 1934.

faq.question: What is Atatürk's status in the Republic of Turkey?
faq.answer: He is revered in Turkey as an independence hero and the founding father of the nation. The Turkish parliament granted him the honorific 'Atatürk', and he is the sole individual featured on Turkish banknotes.

faq.question: When did Atatürk die and what was the cause of death?
faq.answer: He died on November 10, 1938, at the age of 57. He had been diagnosed with cirrhosis of the liver earlier in 1938.

faq.question: What were the major reform policies implemented by Atatürk?
faq.answer: He implemented numerous reforms, including the abolition of the Caliphate, the legislation of secularism, dress reforms, securing women's right to education, the abolition of the Islamic calendar, the ban on polygamy, the shift to the Latin alphabet, and granting women the right to vote.

timeline.event: Marriage of her parents, Harriet 'Rit' Green and Ben Ross
Born Araminta 'Minty' Ross (estimated birth year)
Her father, Ben Ross, is freed from slavery according to a former owner's will
Marries John Tubman
Escapes to Philadelphia following deteriorating health
Escapes with her brothers Ben and Henry, but the brothers turn back
Rescues her niece Kessiah and Kessiah's two children
Returns to Maryland and rescues her brother Moses and two other enslaved people
Returns to Dorchester County to find her husband John, but he has remarried and refuses to leave with her
Leads a group of 11 escaped slaves to Canada
Her father Ben and his brother buy her mother Rit from Eliza Brodess for $20
Rescues her elderly parents and brings them to St. Catharines, Ontario, Canada
Is introduced to the abolitionist John Brown
John Brown reveals his plan to raid Harpers Ferry, Virginia, in Chatham-Kent, Ontario
Buys a small piece of land on the outskirts of Auburn, New York, from Senator William H. Seward for $1,200
Undertakes her final rescue mission, bringing the Ennals family and another group to freedom
Arrives with the Ennals family and others at the home of David and Martha Coffin Wright in Auburn
Heads to the Hilton Head area of South Carolina after the outbreak of the Civil War
President Lincoln issues the Emancipation Proclamation
Executes the Combahee River Raid, rescuing over 700 enslaved people
Stays at a military base for several months after the Confederate surrender before returning home
Marries Nelson Davis
Sarah Bradford publishes the biography 'Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman'
Gets caught up in a gold-smuggling scam
Adopts a baby girl named Gertie
Sarah Bradford publishes 'Harriet, the Moses of Her People'
Undergoes brain surgery at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston
Participates as a keynote speaker at the first meeting of the National Federation of Afro-American Women
Begins receiving a pension for her military service during the Civil War
Donates a parcel of her land to the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
Attends the opening of the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged as an honorary guest
Enters the nursing home named after her
Dies of pneumonia
Earl Conrad publishes 'Harriet Tubman: Negro Soldier and Abolitionist'
