The United States Maritime Commission launches the SS Harriet Tubman, its first Liberty ship named after a Black woman
The US Postal Service issues the Tubman commemorative stamp, the first to honor an African American
Molefi Kete Asante includes Harriet Tubman on his list of '100 Greatest African Americans'
Jean Humez publishes a book about Tubman's life
Kate Larson and Catherine Clinton publish biographies of Tubman
Towson University names a new campus residence hall 'Tubman House'
US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announces that Tubman's portrait will replace Andrew Jackson's on the $20 bill
Posthumously commissioned as a brigadier general in the Maryland National Guard on Veterans Day

keyAchievements.title: Activism in the Abolitionist Movement
keyAchievements.description: Through the 'Underground Railroad', a network of anti-slavery activists, she established 13 safe houses and rescued around 70 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an 'Underground Railroad' Conductor
keyAchievements.description: As a 'conductor' on the Underground Railroad from 1850 to 1860, she ventured into the South and led over 300 Black people to freedom in the North.

keyAchievements.title: Rescuing Family
keyAchievements.description: After escaping to Philadelphia, she returned to Maryland to lead her relatives out of the state, eventually bringing freedom to dozens of enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: Served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Combahee River Raid
keyAchievements.description: Became the first woman to lead an armed assault during the Civil War, commanding the Combahee River Raid and freeing over 700 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: Fought for women's suffrage after the war, attending related conferences and speaking out in cities like New York, Boston, and Washington D.C.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Establishment of a Home for the Aged
keyAchievements.description: Spent her final years in a home for elderly African Americans that she helped establish years earlier. In 1903, she donated part of her land to the church, requesting it be used for a home for 'aged and indigent colored people'.

faq.question: What was Harriet Tubman's original name?
faq.answer: Harriet Tubman's birth name was Araminta Ross.

faq.question: How many enslaved people did Harriet Tubman rescue?
faq.answer: She directly rescued around 70 people in 13 trips via the Underground Railroad, led over 300 to freedom in the North as a 'conductor' between 1850 and 1860, and freed more than 700 through the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Was there a bounty on Harriet Tubman?
faq.answer: There is a rumor of a $40,000 bounty on her head, but this is untrue. Even claims of a $12,000 bounty lack supporting evidence.

faq.question: What role did Harriet Tubman play in the Civil War?
faq.answer: She served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy. She was the first woman to lead an armed assault, freeing over 700 enslaved people in the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Why was Harriet Tubman nicknamed 'Moses'?
faq.answer: As she guided more and more people to freedom from slavery, she became famously known as 'Moses', drawing a parallel to the biblical figure in Exodus who liberated his oppressed people.

faq.question: Will Harriet Tubman appear on the $20 bill?
faq.answer: On April 20, 2016, US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announced that Tubman's portrait would replace former President Andrew Jackson's on the front of the $20 bill.

timeline.event: Born as the second daughter to William Edward Nightingale and Frances Nightingale.
Meets the Swiss socialist Sismondi while traveling in Italy.
Observes a hospital in Alexandria while traveling in Egypt and realizes the importance of formal nursing education.
Studies nursing at a Protestant school in Kaiserswerth, Germany, and becomes the superintendent of a women's hospital in London.
Serves as a military nurse at a field hospital in Scutari during the Crimean War.
Elected as the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.
Establishes the Nightingale Training School for Nurses and authors professional nursing texts.
Awarded the Dame of Grace of the Order of St John (DStJ).
Becomes the first woman to receive the Order of Merit from King Edward VII.
Passes away at the age of 90.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Nursing Profession
keyAchievements.description: By serving as a military nurse in the Crimean War, she popularized the profession of nursing and matured it into a specialized field.

keyAchievements.title: Improving Hospital Hygiene
keyAchievements.description: Implemented discipline and sanitary conditions in the chaotic field hospital at Scutari during the Crimean War, reducing the mortality rate from 42 percent to 2 percent.

keyAchievements.title: Building a Nursing Education System
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for nursing education by establishing the Nightingale Training School for Nurses in 1860 and authoring professional nursing texts.

keyAchievements.title: Proposing the Concept of Intensive Care Units
keyAchievements.description: First proposed the concept of an intensive care unit during the Crimean War, suggesting that critically ill patients be isolated for specialized care.

keyAchievements.title: Statistical Analysis
keyAchievements.description: Used data analysis from the Crimean War to argue for the importance of hospital hygiene, becoming the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Works
keyAchievements.description: Her book 'Notes on Nursing' served as the foundational text for nursing practice and education, and the Nightingale Pledge is still recited by nurses worldwide.

faq.question: Have there been any critical views of the veneration of Nightingale?
faq.answer: In 1999, the British public sector union UNISON opposed the veneration of Nightingale, arguing that it hindered nursing independence and contributed to low wages, while also overshadowing the achievements of other nurses.

faq.question: Were there other notable nurses active during Nightingale's time?
faq.answer: Mary Seacole from Jamaica was active during the same period, but due to racial discrimination, she was unable to work alongside Nightingale and lived in poverty.

timeline.event: Born in Coupvray, Seine-et-Marne, France.
Injured his left eye with an awl in his father's workshop; an infection subsequently caused him to begin losing sight in his right eye as well.
Became completely blind.
Received a scholarship to attend the Royal Institute for Blind Youth in Paris.
Charles Barbier visited the school and introduced 'night writing', a system based on 12 raised dots.
Expanded his own raised-dot system to include math and music, and published it in a book.
Died of pulmonary tuberculosis in Paris at the age of 43.
His remains were moved and interred at the Panthéon in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Braille
keyAchievements.description: Invented braille, a system of writing and reading using 1 to 6 raised dots that can be felt with the fingertips.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion and Publication of Braille
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the braille system to encompass mathematics and music, and published it as a book in 1827.

faq.question: What is Louis Braille most famous for?
faq.answer: He is famous for inventing braille, a tactile writing system consisting of 1 to 6 raised dots that allows the visually impaired to read and write using their fingers.

faq.question: When and where was Louis Braille born?
faq.answer: Louis Braille was born on January 4, 1809, in Coupvray, Seine-et-Marne, France.

faq.question: How did Louis Braille lose his sight?
faq.answer: At the age of 3, he injured his left eye with an awl in his father's workshop. An infection spread to his right eye, leaving him completely blind by the age of 4.

faq.question: How does Louis Braille's system differ from Charles Barbier's 'night writing'?
faq.answer: While Barbier's system used 12 dots to represent sounds, Braille's system used a 6-dot cell corresponding to letters, which made it much easier and faster to read and write.

timeline.event: Born to a peasant family in Domrémy, a village in northeastern France.
At age 13, received divine revelations from Saint Michael, Saint Catherine, and Saint Margaret.
Around the age of 17, sought out Charles VII, gained his trust, and joined the Hundred Years' War.
Underwent a theological examination in Poitiers, which reported that she had 'lived an irreproachable life, was a devout Christian, and possessed the virtues of humility, honesty, and simplicity'.
Arrived at the besieged city of Orléans.
The French army attacked and recaptured the fortress of Saint-Loup.
Advocated for and led the attack on Les Tourelles, the main English stronghold.
Recaptured Jargeau, Meung-sur-Loire, and Beaugency.
Defeated the English army at the Battle of Patay.
Facilitated the coronation of Charles VII as King of France at Reims Cathedral.
Attacked Paris but retreated upon the king's orders.
Recaptured Saint-Pierre-le-Moûtier and was granted a title of nobility.
Captured by Burgundian forces during the siege of Compiègne.
Transferred to Rouen, which was under English control.
Her trial for heresy began in English-occupied Rouen.
Sentenced to burn at the stake by the ecclesiastical court.
Burned at the stake before a crowd in the Place du Vieux-Marché in Rouen.
A retrial for Joan of Arc was initiated at the behest of Pope Callixtus III.
A formal appeal against the charges levied on Joan of Arc was submitted.
The appellate court pronounced her innocent of all charges and declared her a martyr.
Beatified by the Holy See.
Canonized as a saint by Pope Benedict XV.

keyAchievements.title: Decisive Contribution to the French Victory in the Hundred Years' War
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in reversing the tide of the Hundred Years' War by boosting the morale of the French army and leading them to victory in several battles, notably the Siege of Orléans.

keyAchievements.title: Facilitating the Coronation of Charles VII
keyAchievements.description: Through the recapture of Reims, she enabled Charles VII to be crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral, establishing his legitimacy.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Rehabilitation and Canonization
keyAchievements.description: Cleared of all charges and declared a martyr in a posthumous retrial, she was beatified in 1909 and canonized as a saint in 1920.

faq.question: Why was Joan of Arc tried by an ecclesiastical court?
faq.answer: The English accused her of treason and heresy to undermine the legitimacy of Charles VII. Additionally, the Church believed her claims of communicating directly with God disrupted ecclesiastical hierarchy.

faq.question: How did Joan of Arc die?
faq.answer: She was sentenced to death by burning on May 29, 1431, by an ecclesiastical court. The following day, May 30, she was tied to a pillar and burned at the stake before a crowd in the Place du Vieux-Marché in Rouen.

faq.question: Why did Joan of Arc wear men's clothing?
faq.answer: Joan stated that she wore men's clothing to function properly in the male-dominated army and to protect her chastity in case of assault.

faq.question: Why was a retrial held for Joan of Arc?
faq.answer: The retrial was held to clear up any doubts about the legitimacy of Charles VII's claim to the French throne and, at the request of Pope Callixtus III and Joan's mother, to re-examine the validity of her original conviction.

timeline.event: Born on June 14 in Litchfield, Connecticut, USA.
Lived in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Wrote and published a children's geography book under her sister Catharine's name.
Married Calvin Stowe, a seminary professor.
Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.
Published the novel 'Dred'.
Published the novel 'The Minister's Wooing'.
Published the novel 'The Pearl of Orr's Island'.
Published 'Lady Byron Vindicated'.
Published 'Sam Lawson's Oldtown Fireside Stories'.
Published the novel 'Oldtown Folks'.
Died on July 1 at the age of 85 in Hartford.

keyAchievements.title: Writing 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'
keyAchievements.description: Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin', which intensified the conflict of opinion between the North and South, ultimately leading to the Civil War.

keyAchievements.title: Writing a Children's Geography Book
keyAchievements.description: Wrote and published her first book, a children's geography book, in 1833 under her sister Catharine's name.

faq.question: Who was Harriet Beecher Stowe?
faq.answer: She was an American abolitionist and realist author, best known for her anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.

faq.question: What was the impact of 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'?
faq.answer: The novel profoundly influenced the debate on slavery at the time and significantly widened the divide between the North and South, paving the way for the Civil War.

faq.question: Why did Harriet Beecher Stowe become interested in the abolitionist movement?
faq.answer: She witnessed the miserable living conditions of enslaved Black people during a trip to Kentucky, which fueled her passion for the abolitionist movement. Furthermore, the loss of her mother at a young age is said to have evoked a deep sense of empathy within her for enslaved boys and girls separated from their families.

timeline.event: Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Simone de Beauvoir is born in Paris, France.
Presents on Leibniz at the Sorbonne and passes the agrégation in philosophy in second place.
Publishes the novel She Came to Stay (L'Invitée).
Writes Pyrrhus and Cineas (Pyrrhus et Cinéas), an essay on existentialist ethics.
Publishes The Ethics of Ambiguity (Pour Une Morale de L'ambiguïté).
The Second Sex (Le Deuxième Sexe) is published in France.
Publishes the autobiography Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter (Mémoires d'une jeune fille rangée).
Publishes the autobiography The Prime of Life (La Force de l'âge).
Publishes the autobiography Force of Circumstance (La Force des choses).
Writes the essay Old Age (La Vieillesse).
Signs the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.
Publishes the autobiography All Said and Done (Tout compte fait).
Publishes When Things of the Spirit Come First (Quand prime le spirituel), containing stories of her experiences as a woman in her youth.
Writes Adieux: A Farewell to Sartre (La Cérémonie Des Adieux), covering Sartre's later years.
Dies of pneumonia and is buried in the Montparnasse Cemetery in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Writer and Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: A prominent French writer and philosopher who wrote in various fields including novels, philosophical essays, biographies, and autobiographies.

keyAchievements.title: She Came to Stay
keyAchievements.description: A novelized chronicle of the complex relationship between herself, Sartre, Olga Kosakiewicz, and Wanda Kosakiewicz.

keyAchievements.title: The Mandarins
keyAchievements.description: A Goncourt Prize-winning novel set after World War II, depicting surrounding figures like Sartre and Nelson Algren.

keyAchievements.title: The Second Sex
keyAchievements.description: A monumental work containing an in-depth analysis of women's oppression, becoming a cornerstone of modern feminism.

keyAchievements.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
keyAchievements.description: An accessible introduction to French existentialism, possessing a simplicity that contrasts with Sartre's more difficult works.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Editing of Les Temps Modernes
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the political magazine Les Temps Modernes with Sartre and Merleau-Ponty, and worked as an editor until shortly before her death.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Autobiographies
keyAchievements.description: Documented her life through four famous autobiographies, beginning with Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the French Women's Liberation Movement
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.

faq.question: Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
faq.answer: She was a French writer and philosopher who wrote novels, as well as essays, biographies, and autobiographies on philosophy, politics, and social issues.

faq.question: What are her major works?
faq.answer: She wrote metaphysical novels such as She Came to Stay and The Mandarins, and in 1949 wrote The Second Sex, which became a cornerstone of modern feminism.

faq.question: What was Simone de Beauvoir's family background?
faq.answer: She was born to Georges de Beauvoir, a legal secretary, and Françoise Brasseur, and her younger sister Hélène de Beauvoir was a painter.

faq.question: What was her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre?
faq.answer: She met Sartre at the University of Paris. In the 1929 philosophy agrégation, Sartre ranked first and she ranked second, and they maintained a complex lifelong relationship.

faq.question: What was the core argument in The Second Sex?
faq.answer: She argued that men had imposed a false aura of 'mystery' on women, turning them into the social 'Other', and adopting the maxim 'existence precedes essence', she posited that one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

faq.question: How did she participate in the women's liberation movement?
faq.answer: She was active in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 in 1971, demanding the legalization of abortion, which was illegal in France at the time.

timeline.event: Galileo di Vincenzo Bonaiuti de' Galilei is born in Pisa, Tuscany, Italy.
Becomes a professor of mathematics at the University of Pisa.
Writes On Motion (De Motu).
Improves upon a 3x telescope invented in the Netherlands and demonstrates his first telescope to Venetian lawmakers.
Discovers the four largest moons of Jupiter using a telescope and publishes Sidereus Nuncius (Starry Messenger).
Accepts the invitation of Cosimo II, Grand Duke of Tuscany, moves to Florence, and continues his research.
Visits Rome and becomes a member of the Accademia dei Lincei.
Ordered by Cardinal Robert Bellarmine not to defend or teach Copernican astronomy.
Publishes The Assayer, attacking theories based on Aristotelian authority and advancing a mathematical system of scientific thought.
Publishes Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems, which leads to his trial by the Roman Inquisition.
Found guilty by the Inquisition, placed under house arrest, and all his works are placed on the Index of Forbidden Books.
Completes Discourses and Mathematical Demonstrations Relating to Two New Sciences, and becomes completely blind.
Galileo dies.
Pope John Paul II acknowledges that the Galileo trial was unjust and apologizes.

keyAchievements.title: Improvement of the Telescope and Astronomical Observations
keyAchievements.description: Improved the telescope and discovered Jupiter's moons, lunar craters, and sunspots; his observation of the phases of Venus supported the Copernican heliocentric theory.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Laws of Motion
keyAchievements.description: Studied uniformly accelerated motion to establish kinematics, and established the concepts of velocity and acceleration, which laid the foundation for Newton's laws of motion.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Scientific Method
keyAchievements.description: Established universal mathematical laws and the quantitative analysis of empirical facts, leading to the beginning of physics in the modern sense.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Jupiter's Four Largest Moons
keyAchievements.description: Discovered Io, Europa, Callisto, and Ganymede, proving that not all celestial bodies revolve around the Earth, which contradicted the Aristotelian worldview.

faq.question: Did Galileo Galilei actually perform the drop experiment from the Leaning Tower of Pisa?
faq.answer: No, this experiment was a story invented by Viviani, Galileo's biographer and disciple, to elevate him; Galileo refuted Aristotle's theory through logical reasoning.

faq.question: Was Galileo a devout Catholic despite his conflicts with the Church?
faq.answer: Yes, despite his conflict with the Church, he was a devout Roman Catholic, to the point where he once hoped to become a Catholic priest.

faq.question: Is the anecdote true that Galileo muttered 'And yet it moves' after his Inquisition trial?
faq.answer: No, while this widely known anecdote illustrates his passion for seeking scientific truth, there is no reliable evidence that Galileo actually said it.

faq.question: What stance did the Vatican take on Galileo's trial concerning heliocentrism?
faq.answer: In 1992, Pope John Paul II acknowledged the injustice of the Galileo trial and issued an apology; earlier, in 1744, Galileo's book 'Dialogue' was removed from the Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

timeline.event: Born as the third son of Léopold Hugo in Besançon, eastern France.
Fascinated by reading and writing poetry at the Cordeliers boarding school in Paris, vowing to become a great French writer.
Published his first poetry collection, Odes et poésies diverses, and got married.
Published the novel Han d'Islande.
Awarded the Knight of the Legion of Honour by the French royal family in recognition of his merits as a writer.
Published the poetry collection Odes et Ballades and the novel Bug-Jargal.
Directly opposed classical literature and led romantic writers through the publication of the preface to his play Cromwell.
Published Les Orientales and Le Dernier jour d'un condamné.
The "Battle of Hernani" broke out between the romantic and classical schools around the premiere of the play Hernani.
Published the novel Notre-Dame de Paris, the poetry collection Les Feuilles d'automne, and the play Marion Delorme.
Published the play Le roi s'amuse.
Published the plays Lucrèce Borgia and Marie Tudor.
Published the poetry collection Les Chants du crépuscule.
Published the poetry collection Les Voix intérieures.
Published the verse play Ruy Blas.
Published the poetry collection Les Rayons et les Ombres.
Became a member of the Académie Française.
The play Les Burgraves was severely criticized, and his eldest daughter Léopoldine and her husband died in a boat capsizing accident.
Devoted himself to political activities, barely publishing any works.
Solidified his humanitarian tendencies, stimulated by surrounding circumstances including the February Revolution.
Rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.
Published the poetry collection Les Châtiments.
Published the poetry collection Les Contemplations.
Published the first series of the poetry collection La Légende des siècles.
Published the novel Les Misérables.
Published the novel Les Travailleurs de la mer.
Wrote Les Années funestes, depicting the reality of French workers.
Returned to Paris as a defender of republicanism with the fall of Napoleon III due to the Franco-Prussian War.
Published L'Année terrible.
Published the novel Quatrevingt-treize.
Published the second series of La Légende des siècles.
Wrote his will.
Published the third series of La Légende des siècles.
Died, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon in a state funeral.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Romantic Literature
keyAchievements.description: He presented the theory of romantic theater in the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Masterpiece Novels
keyAchievements.description: He solidified his position as a novelist with Notre-Dame de Paris and wrote numerous masterpieces including Les Misérables during his exile.

keyAchievements.title: Social Criticism and Humanitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Through his works, he expressed love for poor workers, emphasized popular art, and solidified his humanitarian tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Major Figure in the French Literary World
keyAchievements.description: He occupied an important position in the French literary world, receiving the Knight of the Legion of Honour in 1825 and becoming a member of the Académie Française in 1841.

faq.question: Who was Victor Hugo?
faq.answer: He was a French poet, novelist, and playwright.

faq.question: What impact did Victor Hugo have on romanticism?
faq.answer: He presented the theory of romantic theater through the preface to his play Cromwell, won the "Battle of Hernani", and led romantic writers.

faq.question: What are Victor Hugo's representative works?
faq.answer: They include the novels Notre-Dame de Paris and Les Misérables.

faq.question: Why was Victor Hugo in exile?
faq.answer: He rebelled against Napoleon III's coup d'état in 1851, was exiled abroad, and began about 19 years of exile.

faq.question: What was Victor Hugo's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: His works were banned by the Catholic Church, and he could not reconcile with Catholicism, even refusing the church's prayers.

faq.question: When did Victor Hugo die, and where is he buried?
faq.answer: He died on May 22, 1885, and his remains were interred in the Panthéon.

timeline.event: Frédéric François Chopin was born.
Chopin's family moved to Warsaw.
Warsaw became part of the constitutional Kingdom of Poland.
Received his first professional music education from Wojciech Żywny.
Began holding public concerts at age 7 and composed two polonaises.
Composed the Polonaise in A-flat major and dedicated it to Żywny.
Performed his own improvisations and part of Moscheles's concerto on the aeolomelodicon.
Performed his Rondo Op. 1 (his first commercially published work) at an aeolopantaleon concert.
Attended the Warsaw Lyceum and studied music theory, figured bass, and composition at the Warsaw Conservatory for 3 years.
Shortly after his youngest sister Emilia died, the family moved to Krakowskie Przedmieście.
Visited Berlin with a family friend.
Composed Souvenir de Paganini after hearing Niccolò Paganini play the violin.
Debuted in Vienna, holding two piano concerts and receiving favorable reviews.
Premiered Piano Concerto No. 2 in F minor, Op. 21.
Left Warsaw.
The November Uprising broke out in Warsaw.
Expressed anguish in his diary upon hearing news of the uprising's suppression while traveling from Vienna to Paris.
Arrived in Paris.
Robert Schumann declared 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius' in a review of the Op. 2 Variations.
Held his Paris debut concert to universal acclaim.
Published the Études Op. 10.
Attended the Lower Rhenish Music Festival with Hiller and met Felix Mendelssohn.
Went to Karlovy Vary to spend his last time with his parents.
Obtained French citizenship.
Met George Sand at a party hosted by Marie d'Agoult.
Stayed with the Wodziński family in Marienbad and Dresden, and proposed to Maria.
Visited London secretly with piano manufacturer Camille Pleyel.
Published the Études Op. 25.
Became lovers with George Sand.
Spent the winter with Sand in Majorca.
Published the Preludes Op. 28.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 2, Op. 35.
Headed to Sand's estate in Nohant to spend the summer.
Last public joint appearance with Liszt.
Wrote a letter to Grzymała about his deteriorating health after a Paris recital.
Composed Piano Sonata No. 3, Op. 58.
The relationship between Sand and Chopin deteriorated.
His relationship with Sand ended after a heated exchange of letters.
Held his last Paris concert with cellist Auguste Franchomme.
Left for London during the French Revolution of 1848.
Queen Victoria and Prince Albert attended his first London performance at Stafford House.
Wrote his last will in Edinburgh.
Held his last public concert at the Guildhall in London.
His older sister Ludwika came to Paris with her husband and daughter.
Died in Paris at the age of 39.
Funeral held at the Madeleine Church in Paris.
Chopin's tombstone was installed.
His older sister Ludwika took Chopin's heart back to Poland.
Julian Fontana compiled and published 23 unpublished piano pieces with 8 additional opus numbers (Op. 66–73).
17 Polish songs were published as Op. 74.
Chopin's music was used in the ballet Les Sylphides (Chopiniana).
Claude Debussy dedicated his piano études to the memory of Chopin.
Results of a visual examination of Chopin's preserved heart were published in the American Journal of Medicine.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Composer
keyAchievements.description: Maintained worldwide fame with his poetic genius based on a professional technique unmatched by anyone of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Specializing in Solo Piano Music
keyAchievements.description: Composed primarily solo piano music, and all his known works feature the piano.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Instrumental Ballade
keyAchievements.description: Created the instrumental ballade as a new musical genre.

keyAchievements.title: Making the Scherzo a Concert Piece
keyAchievements.description: Was the first to compose scherzos as individual concert pieces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Prelude Genre
keyAchievements.description: Established essentially a new genre with his free cycle of preludes (Op. 28).

keyAchievements.title: Utilizing the Poetic Potential of Études
keyAchievements.description: Utilized the poetic potential of the concept of the concert étude in his two sets of études (Op. 10, Op. 25).

keyAchievements.title: Elevating the Status of the Mazurka
keyAchievements.description: Put the mazurka on the European musical map.

keyAchievements.title: New Standards for the Polonaise Form
keyAchievements.description: Set new standards for music in the polonaise form.

keyAchievements.title: Deepening the Nocturne Genre
keyAchievements.description: Elevated the nocturne to a deeper level of sophistication.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing Nationalist Consciousness into Music
keyAchievements.description: Is evaluated as having introduced a new nationalist consciousness into music.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Piano and Composition Schools
keyAchievements.description: Was called the founder of piano and composition schools.

faq.question: When was Chopin born?
faq.answer: Frédéric François Chopin was born on March 1, 1810.

faq.question: What was Chopin's real name?
faq.answer: His real name was Fryderyk Franciszek Chopin.

faq.question: What instrument did Chopin primarily compose for?
faq.answer: Chopin composed primarily solo piano music, and the piano appears in all his works.

faq.question: How many public performances did Chopin give in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He gave a minimal number of public performances, slightly over 30, preferring the more intimate atmosphere of salons.

faq.question: How did Chopin make a living in Paris?
faq.answer: He made a living by selling his compositions and giving piano lessons, which were highly popular at the time.

faq.question: What was George Sand's relationship to Chopin?
faq.answer: George Sand was a French author and Chopin's lover; they became lovers in July 1838.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Chopin and Franz Liszt?
faq.answer: The two were friends and respected each other, but their friendship was unstable and had the characteristics of a love-hate relationship.

faq.question: What was the cause of Chopin's death?
faq.answer: His death certificate listed the cause of death as tuberculosis, while recent research suggests it was highly likely a rare pericarditis caused by chronic tuberculosis complications.

faq.question: Where is Chopin's heart preserved?
faq.answer: His heart was removed by a doctor, preserved in alcohol in an urn, and taken back to Poland by his older sister Ludwika in 1850.

faq.question: Did Chopin give descriptive titles to his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of his Funeral March, the composer never named his instrumental pieces beyond genre and number.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

timeline.event: Born near present-day Melmoth, KwaZulu-Natal, as the illegitimate son of Zulu Chief Senzangakhona.
Took the Zulu throne following the death of his father, with the backing of the Mthethwa paramount chief Dingiswayo.
After Dingiswayo's death, Shaka defeated the rival Ndwandwe clan at the Battle of Gqokli Hill, establishing Zulu dominance.
His mother, Nandi, died, triggering a period of extreme and brutal mourning enforced by Shaka.
Assassinated by his half-brothers Dingane and Mhlangana at his capital, kwaDukuza.
Assassinated by his half-brothers

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Zulu Empire
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the small Zulu clan into a massive, militaristic empire that dominated southern Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Military Innovations
keyAchievements.description: Revolutionized regional warfare by introducing the iklwa (a short stabbing spear), the large cowhide shield, and the highly effective 'buffalo horns' formation.

keyAchievements.title: Social Integration
keyAchievements.description: Integrated conquered tribes into the Zulu nation, replacing traditional tribal loyalties with loyalty to the king and the age-grade military regiments (amabutho).

keyAchievements.title: Development of the 'bull horn' tactic
keyAchievements.description: Developed the 'bull horn' formation tactic that annihilated Zwide's army at the Battle of Gqokli Hill.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of hegemony based on military power
keyAchievements.description: Expanded his hegemony by defeating rivals and incorporating their scattered remnants into his own army based on military power.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Nguni shield
keyAchievements.description: It is believed that he introduced a larger and heavier version of the Nguni shield.

keyAchievements.title: Training in shield tactics
keyAchievements.description: Believed to have taught warriors the tactic of using the left side of their shield to hook the enemy's shield to the right, exposing their ribs.

keyAchievements.title: Forced march training
keyAchievements.description: Frequently subjected his troops to forced marches across hot, rocky terrain at a rapid pace.

keyAchievements.title: Youth logistical support system
keyAchievements.description: Recruited boys over the age of six as apprentice warriors (udibi) to handle logistical support such as carrying food, pots, mats, and spare weapons.

keyAchievements.title: Organization of age-grade regiments
keyAchievements.description: Organized different age groups into regiments, stationed them in special military kraals, and gave them unique names and insignia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of military discipline
keyAchievements.description: Instilled ruthless determination in his army by drilling into them that a cruel fate awaited those who lost courage or were defeated in battle.

keyAchievements.title: Incorporation of conquered tribes
keyAchievements.description: Incorporated the remnants of conquered tribes into his army and used them to conquer other tribes.

faq.question: What is the 'Mfecane'?
faq.answer: The Mfecane (The Crushing) refers to a period of widespread chaos and warfare among indigenous ethnic communities in southern Africa in the early 19th century. While Shaka's expansions contributed to this, modern historians debate the extent to which he was the primary cause versus other factors like drought and colonial pressures.

faq.question: Was Shaka as brutal as he is often portrayed?
faq.answer: He was a brilliant but ruthless leader. Much of his 'bloodthirsty' reputation was exaggerated by early European writers to justify colonialism. However, his harsh discipline and the mass executions following his mother's death demonstrate he could be extremely cruel.

faq.question: What was the 'buffalo horns' formation?
faq.answer: It was a military tactic Shaka perfected. It consisted of a strong center (the 'chest') to pin the enemy, while two flanking wings (the 'horns') encircled them, and a reserve force (the 'loins') waited behind to pursue fleeing enemies or reinforce weak spots.

faq.question: How did Shaka Zulu die?
faq.answer: He was assassinated in September 1828 by his half-brothers Dingane kaSenzangakhona and Mhlangana, and Mbopa kaSithayi.

faq.question: What was Shaka Zulu's role in the Mfecane?
faq.answer: Shaka's reign coincided with the Mfecane period, and although his military expansion caused massive migrations and turmoil, his exact role remains controversial.

timeline.event: Born in Mainz.
Gutenberg's father was exiled by the citizens and fled to Strasbourg.
After his father passed away, Gutenberg also moved to Strasbourg.
Used movable metal type printing.
Returned to Mainz.
Succeeded in metal type printing and opened a printing press in Mainz.
Improved to smaller and more advanced type, and printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes.
Printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines per page.
Fust filed a lawsuit against Gutenberg with the Archbishop of Mainz.
The 'Mainz Psalter' was published by the Fust-Schöffer printing press.
Death.

keyAchievements.title: Use of movable metal type printing
keyAchievements.description: A German goldsmith who used movable metal type printing around 1440.

keyAchievements.title: Type design and spread of mass production technology
keyAchievements.description: Spread type design and type mass production technology throughout Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Combination of printing technologies
keyAchievements.description: Combined movable metal type printing technology with the use of oil-based ink and wooden printing presses.

keyAchievements.title: Adoption of type manufacturing materials and methods
keyAchievements.description: Used an alloy as the material for making type and adopted a casting method.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the Gutenberg Bible
keyAchievements.description: Printed the first 36-line Latin Bible, the <Gutenberg Bible>, using Gothic type.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 42-line Old Testament
keyAchievements.description: Around 1453, after improving the type, he printed a 42-line Old Testament in two volumes, which was highly acclaimed for its excellent printing quality.

keyAchievements.title: Printing of the 'Biblia Sacra'
keyAchievements.description: In 1455, printed the 'Biblia Sacra' with 42 lines on each page.

faq.question: What is Gutenberg's real name?
faq.answer: His real name is Johannes Gensfleisch, and Gutenberg is a common appellation.

faq.question: When was Gutenberg born?
faq.answer: Gutenberg is believed to have been born in Mainz in either 1397 or 1398.

faq.question: What are Gutenberg's major achievements?
faq.answer: His major achievements include spreading type design and mass production technology in Europe, and combining this technology with oil-based ink and the use of the wooden printing press.

faq.question: What books did Gutenberg print?
faq.answer: He printed Catholic indulgences, the first 36-line Latin <Gutenberg Bible>, and the 42-line 'Biblia Sacra' in 1455.

faq.question: What impact did Gutenberg's printing press have on human history?
faq.answer: His invention allowed books to spread massively across Europe, triggering an information revolution and laying the foundation for the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the humanist movement.

timeline.event: Born in Oak Park, Illinois.
Attended Oak Park and River Forest High School.
Published his first article in the school newspaper, 'The Trapeze'.
Severely wounded by mortar fire while serving as an ambulance driver for the Italian Red Cross, and received the Silver Medal of Military Valor.
Returned to the United States and began working as a freelancer and staff writer for the 'Toronto Star Weekly'.
Married Hadley Richardson, moved to Paris, and worked as a foreign correspondent for the 'Toronto Star'.
Met Ezra Pound at Sylvia Beach's bookstore, Shakespeare and Company.
Published his first book 'Three Stories and Ten Poems' and his son Jack Hemingway was born.
Published 'In Our Time' and met F. Scott Fitzgerald.
Published his first novel 'The Sun Also Rises'.
Divorced Hadley Richardson, married Pauline Pfeiffer, and published the short story collection 'Men Without Women'.
His son Patrick was born, and he received news of his father's suicide.
Published the novel 'A Farewell to Arms'.
Broke his right arm in a car accident.
His third son, Gregory Hemingway, was born.
Went on a safari trip to Kenya with Pauline.
Purchased the fishing boat Pilar.
Published 'Green Hills of Africa'.
Traveled to Spain to cover the Spanish Civil War for the North American Newspaper Alliance, and published 'To Have and Have Not'.
Began living at the Hotel Ambos Mundos in Havana, Cuba.
Married Martha Gellhorn and published the novel 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'.
Traveled to China with Martha.
Stayed in London and other parts of Europe, and participated as a journalist in the Normandy landings and the liberation of Paris.
Divorced Martha Gellhorn.
Married Mary Welsh.
Awarded the Bronze Star Medal for his bravery during World War II.
Published the novel 'Across the River and into the Trees'.
Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' and embarked on a second trip to Africa.
Survived two plane crashes in Africa and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Left Cuba and purchased a house on the outskirts of Ketchum, Idaho.
Left Cuba for New York and was admitted to the Mayo Clinic.
Returned to Ketchum after being discharged from the Mayo Clinic, and committed suicide with a shotgun on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: Stylistic innovation
keyAchievements.description: Profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction with a concise, understated writing style known as the Iceberg Theory.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

keyAchievements.title: Pulitzer Prize
keyAchievements.description: Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' in 1952.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of major works
keyAchievements.description: Published numerous masterpieces including 'The Sun Also Rises' (1926), 'A Farewell to Arms' (1929), 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' (1940), and 'The Old Man and the Sea' (1951).

keyAchievements.title: Bronze Star Medal
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Bronze Star Medal in 1947 for his bravery during World War II.

keyAchievements.title: Literary influence
keyAchievements.description: Greatly influenced the overall style of American literature and was inducted into the Chicago Literary Hall of Fame in 2012.

faq.question: What is Ernest Hemingway's signature writing style?
faq.answer: Hemingway profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction by using a concise, understated writing style known as the 'Iceberg Theory'.

faq.question: When did Hemingway win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

faq.question: What are some of Hemingway's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Sun Also Rises', 'A Farewell to Arms', 'For Whom the Bell Tolls', and 'The Old Man and the Sea'.

faq.question: How did Hemingway's life end?
faq.answer: On July 2, 1961, he committed suicide with a shotgun in Ketchum, Idaho.

faq.question: What are the main themes of Hemingway's works?
faq.answer: Love, war, the wilderness, and defeat frequently appear as main themes in his works, with nature often depicted as a place of rebirth and rest.

timeline.event: Born in Afshana near Bukhara as the child of Abdullah ibn Al-Hasan, a tax collector of the Samanid Empire, and Sitara.
At the age of five, his family moved to Bukhara, and he entered a private school in Bukhara.
By the age of ten, he could already recite the Quran and many Arabic literary works from memory.
Started treating patients at the age of sixteen.
Studied on his own at eighteen and came to understand Aristotelian philosophy through Al-Farabi's commentaries.
At the age of eighteen, he wrote his first work, 'The Compendium of Various Sciences', for his neighbor Al-Aruzi.
The Samanid Empire fell due to attacks by the Ghaznavids and the Karakhanids.
His father Abdullah passed away.
At twenty-one, he wrote and presented the 20-volume encyclopedia 'The Book of Fair Judgment' for the jurist Al-Barqi.
Left Bukhara at twenty-two and embarked on a wandering journey.
Was employed by Mamun II, the ruler of Urgench in the Khwarezm region, became an official, and started writing 'The Canon of Medicine'.
Moved to Rey near Tehran and wrote about 30 short essays.
Completed 'The Canon of Medicine', which he had been writing for some time.
After Shams al-Dawla passed away, Ibn Sina abandoned his official post and went into seclusion.
Was imprisoned by Sama al-Dawla, the new ruler of Hamadan.
Isfahan was attacked by Mas'ud I, the ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, and Ibn Sina's belongings, including his collection of books, were looted.
Died in Hamadan.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of philosophy and medicine
keyAchievements.description: Compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy and medicine, and had a profound impact on European medicine and philosophy by integrating Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Canon of Medicine
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time and was used as a standard medical text in Europe until the 17th century.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Book of Healing
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Book of Healing', an encyclopedia covering all fields of study except ethics and politics.

keyAchievements.title: Natural science research
keyAchievements.description: Denied the possibility of alchemical transmutation of metals, and researched various fields of natural science including mathematics, physics, astronomy, and mineralogy.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European academia
keyAchievements.description: Influenced Thomas Aquinas and became the catalyst for the revival of Aristotelianism in Europe in the late 12th century.

faq.question: How is Ibn Sina evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: A Persian philosopher, physician, and scientist, he is regarded as one of the greatest medical scholars of the Middle Ages and the finest intellectual produced by the Islamic world.

faq.question: What is Ibn Sina's major medical work?
faq.answer: He authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time, and this book was used as the basic textbook of European medicine until the 17th century.

faq.question: What philosophical contributions did Ibn Sina make?
faq.answer: He compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy, integrated Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism, and greatly influenced European medicine and philosophy.

faq.question: What was Ibn Sina's stance on alchemy?
faq.answer: He denied the possibility of metal transmutation believed by alchemists of the time, arguing that the differences between metals are profound and that precious metals created through alchemy are merely imitations.

timeline.event: Birth of Francois-Dominique Toussaint (Toussaint Louverture)
Hearing news of the French Revolution, black slaves demand the application of the principles of 'Liberty, Equality, Fraternity' in Saint-Domingue
A slave revolution breaks out in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, and Toussaint becomes one of its leaders
Leads to an agreement where France abolishes slavery in the colony
Successfully defends against invasions by British and Spanish forces
The National Convention of the French First Republic declares the abolition of black slavery in all colonies
Governs the colony with the consent of the French governor
Enacts a constitution to permanently abolish slavery and declares that all people are equal regardless of skin color
Napoleon I sends a large army to regain control of the colony and reinstate slavery
Toussaint is captured after agreeing to attend peace talks
Dies in prison while incarcerated in France
The people, inheriting Toussaint's spirit, liberate Port-au-Prince and adopt a declaration of independence
The Republic of Haiti is declared

keyAchievements.title: Led the Haitian Independence Movement
keyAchievements.description: Ended slavery and led the movement for the independence of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

keyAchievements.title: Served as French General and Colonial Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Served as a general in the French army and colonial ruler of Saint-Domingue.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Slave Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the leaders of the slave revolution that occurred in Saint-Domingue in 1791.

keyAchievements.title: Led negotiations for the abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Led an agreement in which France abolished slavery in the colony on the condition that he defeated the British and Spanish forces.

keyAchievements.title: Enacted Constitution and declared abolition of slavery
keyAchievements.description: Enacted a constitution in 1801 that abolished slavery forever and declared all people equal regardless of skin color.

faq.question: Who was Toussaint Louverture?
faq.answer: He was a Haitian revolutionary and independence fighter who ended slavery and led the independence movement of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.

faq.question: What is Toussaint Louverture's real name?
faq.answer: It is Francois-Dominique Toussaint.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture become a revolutionary?
faq.answer: He became a revolutionary by teaching himself French and reading the books of Enlightenment thinkers such as Voltaire.

faq.question: When did the slave revolution led by Toussaint Louverture occur?
faq.answer: It occurred on August 22, 1791, in the French colony of Saint-Domingue.

faq.question: How did Toussaint Louverture die?
faq.answer: In late 1802, he was captured after responding to a peace talk proposal from the French commander, was imprisoned in France, and died in prison on April 7, 1803.

faq.question: What happened to Haiti after Toussaint Louverture's death?
faq.answer: Jean-Jacques Dessalines became a revolutionary and inherited Toussaint's spirit, liberated Port-au-Prince and adopted a declaration of independence in 1803, and declared the Republic of Haiti in 1804.

timeline.event: Born as the second child of Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to care for her mother on her deathbed
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to emancipate herself from her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters'; decided to become an author
Published her first novel 'Mary: A Fiction'; published her only children's book 'Original Stories from Real Life'
Wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' in response to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France'
Wrote her most famous and influential work 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'; left for Paris in December and returned a month before Louis XVI's execution
France declared war on Britain
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife to obtain American citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
The Jacobins fell from power
'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution', written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
Left France
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but Imlay refused to reconcile
Attempted suicide with laudanum but was saved by Imlay
Letters written to Imlay during her Scandinavian trip were compiled and published as 'Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark'
Moved to Somers Town, northwest London, with William Godwin
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished work 'Maria' was published posthumously
Her husband William Godwin published 'Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)
keyAchievements.description: The first feminist philosophical work arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men, but appear to be only because they lack education, advocating for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights.

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' (1790)
keyAchievements.description: A rebuttal to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France', and the first political and feminist writing to attack aristocracy, defend republicanism, and criticize Burke's gender-biased language.

keyAchievements.title: 'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution' (1794)
keyAchievements.description: A book that outlined the early history of the French Revolution, countered the counter-revolutionary atmosphere in Britain, and argued that the revolution was the result of social, economic, and political crises.

keyAchievements.title: 'Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark' (1796)
keyAchievements.description: A travelogue containing reflections on the society and people of Scandinavia, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay, exploring the value of subjective experience and imagination, and advocating for women's liberation and education.

keyAchievements.title: Educational writings
keyAchievements.description: Argued through 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters' (1787) and children's literature 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1788) that children should be taught to think and receive an education that fosters middle-class ethos.

faq.question: What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
faq.answer: Her most famous book is 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman', published in 1792.

faq.question: What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
faq.answer: She argued that women are not inherently inferior to men, but only appear to be due to a lack of education, and therefore should receive quality education.

faq.question: Are any of Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters famous?
faq.answer: Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel 'Frankenstein'.

faq.question: With which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft associate?
faq.answer: She associated with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and notably married William Godwin.

faq.question: What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Reigned as the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.
Recognized as the successor to the throne for his merit in defending Cusco during the Chanka invasion.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for the Inca Empire
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for developing the small Kingdom of Cusco into the massive Inca Empire ruling all of western South America.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of imperial territory
keyAchievements.description: During his reign, Collasuyu and Chinchaysuyu, two of the four quarters of the Inca Empire, were brought under the empire's control.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion and overhaul of the capital Cusco
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Cusco from a quiet provincial city into a place fitting as the capital of the empire through massive expansion.

keyAchievements.title: Renovation of Coricancha
keyAchievements.description: Overhauled various buildings of the Inca Empire era, including the renovation of Coricancha, the Temple of the Sun.

keyAchievements.title: Implementation of forced resettlement policy
keyAchievements.description: Implemented a colonization policy of forcibly moving residents from the center of the empire to borderlands to pioneer frontier regions.

faq.question: What does Pachacuti's name mean?
faq.answer: In Quechua, it means 'He who shakes time and space' or 'Earth Shaker', and 'Yupanqui' means 'honor'.

faq.question: What was Pachacuti's position in the Inca Empire?
faq.answer: He was the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.

faq.question: Who was Pachacuti's father?
faq.answer: His father was Viracocha Inca.

faq.question: How did Pachacuti become the successor to the throne?
faq.answer: He was recognized as the successor to the throne around 1438 for his merit in defending Cusco alone when his father and brother fled during the Chanka invasion.

faq.question: Who is presumed to have built Machu Picchu?
faq.answer: Most archaeologists presume that Pachacuti built Machu Picchu.

timeline.event: Born as the only son of Cambyses I, King of Anshan of Persian descent, and Mandane, daughter of King Astyages of Media
Rebelled against his maternal grandfather Astyages, starting a war with Media
Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana
Marched to Sardis, the capital of Lydia
Defeated King Croesus of Lydia and captured Sardis
The Greek Ionian cities on the Aegean coast submitted to Persia
Invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire and captured Babylon
Killed in action during a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea

keyAchievements.title: Construction of the great Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: Grew Persia into a vast empire encompassing most of Southwest and Central Asia and reaching India.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Median Empire
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana in 549 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Lydia
keyAchievements.description: Conquered Lydia by capturing its capital Sardis in 546 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Neo-Babylonia
keyAchievements.description: Invaded Neo-Babylonia and captured Babylon in 539 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Liberation of Jews and permission to rebuild the Temple
keyAchievements.description: Liberated the Jews held captive in Babylon, allowing them to return to Jerusalem and rebuild their sacred Temple in Jerusalem.

keyAchievements.title: Policy of religious tolerance
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a policy of religious tolerance and inclusion to harmonize the multi-ethnic Persian Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Cyrus Cylinder
keyAchievements.description: Left behind the Cyrus Cylinder, known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery.

faq.question: Where does the name Cyrus originate?
faq.answer: Ancient Greek historians recorded that the name Cyrus originated from 'Kuros' meaning sun, and it is related to the meaning 'like the sun' in Old Persian.

faq.question: How is Cyrus mentioned in the Bible?
faq.answer: He is recorded as 'Koresh' in the Old Testament, and appears as the king who liberated the Jews from the Babylonian captivity and allowed them to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem.

faq.question: How did Cyrus die?
faq.answer: According to ancient historians, he was killed in a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea. Herodotus recorded that he died in a battle with the queen of the Massagetae.

faq.question: What is the Cyrus Cylinder?
faq.answer: It is known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery, and is currently housed in Room 52 of the British Museum.

faq.question: Where is the tomb of Cyrus?
faq.answer: It is located in Pasargadae and is registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

timeline.event: Born in Vienna, Austria.
Obtained a Ph.D. from the University of Vienna.
Married Annemarie (Anny) Bertel.
Moved to the University of Zurich.
Published the paper 'Quantization as an Eigenvalue Problem' containing the Schrödinger equation.
Succeeded Max Planck at Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Left Germany in opposition to Nazism.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics along with Paul Dirac.
Proposed the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
Fired in Graz after the Austrian annexation, fled to Italy, and later relocated to Dublin.
Became the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Authored 'What Is Life?'.
Became a naturalized Irish citizen.
Retired in Dublin.
Returned to Vienna.
Died of tuberculosis in Vienna at the age of 73.
The Department of Physics at Trinity College Dublin recommended removing his name from a lecture theatre considering his history of sexual abuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Schrödinger equation
