keyAchievements.description: Developed an equation providing a way to calculate a system's wave function and how it changes dynamically over time, thereby developing the fundamental results of quantum mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Physics
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'What Is Life?'
keyAchievements.description: Viewed life phenomena from the perspective of physics and addressed the problem of genetics, inspiring the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Schrödinger's cat thought experiment
keyAchievements.description: A famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics, and one of his most enduring legacies.

keyAchievements.title: Research on unified field theory
keyAchievements.description: Made several attempts to construct a unified field theory integrating gravity, electromagnetism, and nuclear forces.

keyAchievements.title: Research on color theory
keyAchievements.description: Showed a strong interest in psychology, particularly color perception and colorimetry, and published a series of papers in this field.

faq.question: For what achievement did Erwin Schrödinger win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

faq.question: What is Schrödinger's cat?
faq.answer: It is a famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.

faq.question: What books did Schrödinger author?
faq.answer: He authored 'What Is Life?', which includes discussions on the genetic code of living organisms and negentropy.

faq.question: When and where was Schrödinger born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born in Vienna, Austria on August 12, 1887, and died of tuberculosis in Vienna on January 4, 1961, at the age of 73.

faq.question: Why did Schrödinger leave Germany?
faq.answer: He left Germany in 1933 because he despised the anti-Semitism of the Nazis.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

keyAchievements.title: Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Laid a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena, established electromagnetism, and proved that light is an electromagnetic wave.

keyAchievements.title: Maxwell's equations
keyAchievements.description: Mathematically organized existing electromagnetic theories and established Maxwell's equations, the differential equations that form the basis of electromagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Kinetic theory of gases
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the velocity distribution law of gas molecules and introduced probabilistic concepts, laying the foundation for statistical mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Color theory and color photography
keyAchievements.description: Produced the first color photograph in 1861 by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Saturn's rings
keyAchievements.description: Showed that Saturn's rings must be composed of small particles to be stable, a prediction proven by the Voyager spacecraft in the 1980s.

faq.question: Who was James Clerk Maxwell?
faq.answer: A British theoretical physicist and mathematician born in Edinburgh, Scotland, recognized as the founder of electromagnetism for establishing a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena.

faq.question: What is Maxwell's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Laying a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena and establishing electromagnetism, as well as presenting 'Maxwell's equations' that mathematically structured this.

faq.question: What contribution did Maxwell make to color photography?
faq.answer: In 1861, he created the first color photograph by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

faq.question: When and where was Maxwell born, and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born on June 13, 1831, in Edinburgh, Scotland, and died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5, 1879, at the age of 48.

keyAchievements.description: After her husband's death, she ruled the Palmyrene Empire as regent and expanded its territory by conquering Roman Egypt.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of an independent state
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to establish an independent state from the Roman Empire by capturing large areas of Roman territory, including the Levant and Asia Minor.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Imperial titles
keyAchievements.description: Declared her son Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and elevated herself to Empress of Palmyra.

faq.question: Who was Zenobia?
faq.answer: A noblewoman and ruler of the Palmyrene Empire, who acted as regent after her husband Odaenathus was assassinated, bringing the empire to its peak before clashing with the Roman Empire and eventually leading to its downfall.

faq.question: What territories did Zenobia conquer?
faq.answer: She successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt, the Levant, and Asia Minor.

faq.question: How did Zenobia's rule come to an end?
faq.answer: She was defeated by the newly ascended Roman Emperor Aurelian, taken to Rome as a captive, and the Palmyrene Empire fell.

faq.question: 제노비아는 어떻게 팔미라 제국의 통치자가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 267년 오데나투스 황제 암살 후 아들 바발라투스를 후계자로 임명하며 팔미라 제국의 공동 통치자가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 제노비아의 최후는 어떻게 되었나요?
faq.answer: 274년 로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에 포로로 끌려왔으나, 그 이후의 정확한 생사는 불명확합니다.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of memorable characters
keyAchievements.description: Created some of the world's most famous fictional characters, including Ebenezer Scrooge, Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for social reform
keyAchievements.description: Strongly criticized the social contradictions, poverty, and child labor issues brought about by the Industrial Revolution through his novels, contributing to social reform.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of the serialization format
keyAchievements.description: Greatly contributed to the popularization of the novel serialization format by publishing novels in weekly or monthly installments, receiving tremendous response from readers.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Christmas
keyAchievements.description: Often referred to as 'The Man Who Invented Christmas,' his work 'A Christmas Carol' played a significant role in establishing the modern culture and concept of Christmas.

faq.question: What is Charles Dickens's most representative work?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'Oliver Twist', 'A Christmas Carol', 'David Copperfield', 'A Tale of Two Cities', and 'Great Expectations'.

faq.question: What was Dickens's childhood like?
faq.answer: His father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a blacking factory at the age of 12. This harsh experience deeply influenced his later works.

faq.question: Why did Dickens's novels usually come out in serial form?
faq.answer: He published them in weekly or monthly magazines to make his works accessible to the public at an affordable price, and this serialization method became very popular during the Victorian era.

faq.question: Did Dickens's works actually influence society?
faq.answer: Yes, his vivid descriptions of the plight of the poor and the poor conditions of child labor raised public awareness and contributed to real social reforms.

faq.question: How did Dickens die?
faq.answer: He died of a stroke at his home, Gad's Hill Place, on June 9, 1870, at the age of 58.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Australia and New Zealand
keyAchievements.description: Was the first European to encounter the eastern coastline of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and completed the first recorded circumnavigation of New Zealand.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of accurate maps
keyAchievements.description: Produced maps of unprecedented accuracy with his excellent seamanship and surveying skills, changing the shape of the world map.

keyAchievements.title: Scurvy prevention
keyAchievements.description: Significantly reduced the incidence of scurvy among his crew by enforcing strict hygiene and providing diets including foods like sauerkraut.

keyAchievements.title: Search for the Northwest Passage and Antarctica
keyAchievements.description: Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time on his second voyage, and explored the Bering Strait in search of the Northwest Passage on his third voyage.

keyAchievements.title: 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 뉴펀들랜드와 뉴질랜드의 해도를 제작했으며, 특히 뉴펀들랜드 해안의 대규모의 정확한 지도를 처음으로 만들었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 괴혈병 사망자 없는 세계 일주

keyAchievements.title: 영국 식민지 개척 기여

keyAchievements.title: 대항해시대의 종결
keyAchievements.description: 소위 대항해시대의 끝을 장식한 인물로, 그의 탐험을 전후하여 세계의 거의 모든 지역이 유럽인에게 알려지게 되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 뉴질랜드 해안 지도 제작 및 해협 발견
keyAchievements.description: 뉴질랜드 해안의 거의 완벽한 지도를 제작하고 뉴질랜드 남북섬을 나누는 쿡 해협을 발견했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남극권 최초 돌입

keyAchievements.title: 남방 대륙 탐험 종지부

keyAchievements.title: 정확한 경도 결정

keyAchievements.title: 세계 일주 항해 일지 작성
keyAchievements.description: 자필 원고로 세계 일주 항해 일지를 남겼습니다(제2 항해).

faq.question: What is Captain James Cook's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: Leading three Pacific voyages, discovering the eastern coast of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and mapping the Pacific Ocean in great detail.

faq.question: Did James Cook discover Australia?
faq.answer: He was not the first European to discover Australia. Dutch explorers had reached the western coast earlier, but Cook was the first to explore and map the eastern coast.

faq.question: How did Captain Cook die?
faq.answer: During his third voyage in 1779, after returning to Hawaii, a conflict arose with the indigenous people over a stolen boat, and he was killed during the altercation.

faq.question: Did Captain Cook find the Northwest Passage?
faq.answer: No, he explored up to the Bering Strait but could not find a navigable Northwest Passage because he was blocked by ice.

faq.question: What was Captain Cook's relationship with the indigenous people like?
faq.answer: He generally tried to maintain peaceful relations, but there were also cultural misunderstandings and violent clashes, a legacy that remains controversial today.

faq.question: 그의 첫 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 왕립 학회는 금성 일면통과 관측을 위해 그를 남태평양에 파견했습니다.

faq.question: 그의 두 번째 항해의 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 남방 대륙(테라 아우스트랄리스)을 발견하기 위해 왕립 학회에 의해 다시 파견되었습니다.

faq.question: 그의 세 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 북극해를 빠져나와 태평양과 대서양을 잇는 북서 항로를 탐색하는 것이었습니다.

timeline.event: December 10: Emily Elizabeth Dickinson was born in Amherst, Massachusetts, USA.
May 15: Died at the age of fifty-five.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry Creation
keyAchievements.description: Wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

keyAchievements.title: Main Themes of Her Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Created poems with main themes including love, death, parting, the soul, and heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Lack of Public Recognition in Life
keyAchievements.description: Was largely unrecognized by the public during her lifetime, with only about 4 of her poems published in collections.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Exchanges in Life
keyAchievements.description: Consistently exchanged poems through correspondence with a few poets and intellectuals who recognized her talent during her lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Recognition of Genius
keyAchievements.description: Her genius was widely recognized after her death when her younger sister, Lavinia Norcross Dickinson, published her collections of poems.

keyAchievements.title: Musical Reinterpretation of Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Her poems have been set to music by renowned composers such as Aaron Copland.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson recognized by the public during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not recognized by the public during her lifetime, and only 4 of her poems were published.

faq.question: What themes did Emily Dickinson's poetry primarily deal with?
faq.answer: Her poetry mainly dealt with themes of love, death, separation, the soul, and heaven.

faq.question: How many poems did Emily Dickinson write?
faq.answer: She wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson a reclusive poet?
faq.answer: She is known as a reclusive poet who strictly refrained from going out, but as a child, she had a lively personality, going out to the fields every day and playing with neighborhood children.

keyAchievements.title: Important Writer of the Victorian Era
keyAchievements.description: Worked as a novelist, poet, journalist, and translator, being one of the most important writers of the Victorian era.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Major Novels
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a total of 7 novels, including 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda', showcasing realism and psychological insight.

keyAchievements.title: Translation of Philosophical Books
keyAchievements.description: Translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' (1846) and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity' (1854).

keyAchievements.title: Editorial Work at 'The Westminster Review'
keyAchievements.description: Worked as the assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review' from 1851 to mid-1854, carrying out practical publishing tasks and contributing many essays and reviews.

keyAchievements.title: Overcoming Prejudice Through a Pen Name
keyAchievements.description: Used the pen name 'George Eliot' to escape prejudice against female writers and to have her works evaluated objectively.

faq.question: What was George Eliot's real name?
faq.answer: George Eliot's real name was Mary Ann Evans.

faq.question: What was the main reason George Eliot used a pen name?
faq.answer: To escape the prejudice that female writers only write lighthearted romance novels, to be evaluated separately from her role as an editor and critic, and to avoid scandals regarding her private life.

faq.question: What are George Eliot's representative novel works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda'.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of her novels?
faq.answer: Most of her novels are set in rural England and are famous for their realism and psychological insight.

faq.question: What major works did George Eliot translate?
faq.answer: She translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.

Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.

Published 'Mansfield Park'.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

keyAchievements.title: Queen of France and England
keyAchievements.description: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

keyAchievements.title: Queen Mother of Two English Kings
keyAchievements.description: She became the Queen Mother of Richard I and King John of England.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Patronage and Dissemination
keyAchievements.description: Patronized troubadours and literary figures, spreading the glamorous and refined courtly culture of southern France to France and England.

keyAchievements.title: Inheritance of Vast Estates
keyAchievements.description: Inherited the vast estates of the Duchy of Aquitaine and the County of Poitiers from her father.

keyAchievements.title: Regency Rule
keyAchievements.description: Ruled as regent when her son Richard I participated in the Third Crusade.

keyAchievements.title: Led Ransom Negotiations
keyAchievements.description: Traveled to the Holy Roman Empire to negotiate the ransom when Richard I was held captive.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Maritime Law
keyAchievements.description: Introduced maritime law to her lands in Oleron and to England.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Trade Customs
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in developing trade customs between the trading ports of the Crusader states and Constantinople.

keyAchievements.title: Support for Her Children
keyAchievements.description: Lived into the reign of her youngest son, John, continuing to support her sons.

keyAchievements.title: Arranged French Royal Marriage
keyAchievements.description: Selected Blanche of Castile as the bride for Prince Louis of France and arranged their marriage.

faq.question: What is Eleanor's estimated year of birth?
faq.answer: Based on records indicating she was 82 at the time of her death in 1204, she is estimated to have been born in 1122.

faq.question: Who was Eleanor married to?
faq.answer: She was the Queen of King Louis VII of France, but after their divorce, she became the Queen of King Henry II of England.

faq.question: Whom did Eleanor patronize?
faq.answer: Influenced by the Poitiers region, a center of 12th-century romantic literature, she patronized troubadours and literary figures.

faq.question: For how long was Eleanor imprisoned?
faq.answer: She was imprisoned from 1173 until the death of Henry II in 1189.

faq.question: Which of Eleanor's children became Kings of England?
faq.answer: Among the sons she had with Henry II, Richard I and King John became Kings of England.

faq.question: Is it true that Eleanor presided over 'Courts of Love'?
faq.answer: Historian Alison Weir concluded that this was a literary invention by Andreas Capellanus, and it is not mentioned in any sources other than Capellanus's book.

keyAchievements.title: Domestic and Military Reforms
keyAchievements.description: Promoted the modernization of Austria by carrying out domestic reforms with the appointment of Haugwitz and military reforms led by Daun.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Appointed Kaunitz to forge an alliance with France, thereby ending the hostilities that had lasted since Maximilian I and achieving the Diplomatic Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Compulsory Education
keyAchievements.description: Established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools nationwide ahead of other countries, greatly raising the intellectual level of the populace.

keyAchievements.title: Prohibition and Crackdown on Prostitution
keyAchievements.description: Banned prostitution and had sex workers, pimps, and brokers arrested, whipped, and sent to labor camps for forced labor.

keyAchievements.title: Strengthening of Military Power
keyAchievements.description: Significantly increased Austria's military strength by establishing a military administration committee, consolidating the Court Council of War, and adopting general conscription.

faq.question: Was Maria Theresa an empress?
faq.answer: Officially, she was the Empress Consort, not the Holy Roman Empress regnant, but since her husband's political power was minimal, she was practically recognized as the Empress by her contemporaries as the de facto ruler.

faq.question: What children did Maria Theresa have?
faq.answer: She had 16 children with Francis Stephen of Lorraine (5 sons and 11 daughters), including Joseph II, Leopold II, and Marie Antoinette.

faq.question: Did Maria Theresa reclaim Silesia?
faq.answer: She attempted to recapture Silesia through the War of the Austrian Succession and the Seven Years' War, but ultimately failed and recognized Prussia's possession of it.

faq.question: How did Maria Theresa become the ruler of the Habsburg dynasty?
faq.answer: Because her father Charles VI did not have a son, she was proclaimed the heiress of the House of Habsburg by the Pragmatic Sanction of 1713 and inherited his territories upon his death.

faq.question: What was Maria Theresa's education policy?
faq.answer: She established compulsory education by setting up elementary schools across the country before other nations, and distributed uniform textbooks to raise the intellectual level of the people.

Became Physician to King James I.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Theory of Blood Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Anatomical Exercise on the Motion of the Heart and Blood in Animals', proposing the theory of blood circulation and proving it through modern scientific methods.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Embryology
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'On the Generation of Animals' and argued that all animals originate from an egg.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Heart and Blood Vessels
keyAchievements.description: Confirmed that the valves between the atria and ventricles, and in the veins, allow blood to flow in only one direction, and discovered that the ventricles act as a pump.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Pulmonary Circulation
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the pulmonary circulation starting from the vena cava and leading to the arterial system.

faq.question: What was William Harvey's primary research method?
faq.answer: Harvey followed a modern scientific research framework by presenting and refuting the contradictions of existing theories, formulating new hypotheses, and verifying them through experiments.

faq.question: Was William Harvey's theory of blood circulation accepted at the time of publication?
faq.answer: His theory of blood circulation was not accepted at the time; even his mentor, Fabricius, allegedly criticized him.

faq.question: What were the main factors that influenced William Harvey's research?
faq.answer: His research was influenced by the public anatomy lectures at the University of Padua, his teacher Fabricius's experiment-based research methods, and Aristotle's microcosm theory.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Foundations of Biological Taxonomy
keyAchievements.description: Made decisive contributions to establishing the foundations of biological taxonomy, earning the title 'Father of modern botany'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Binomial Nomenclature
keyAchievements.description: Established binomial nomenclature consisting of genus and species names and formalized it by using it consistently in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Sexual System of Classification
keyAchievements.description: Created a sexual system for plants, categorizing them into classes and orders based on the number of stamens and pistils.

keyAchievements.title: Author of Major Taxonomic Works
keyAchievements.description: Authored major works such as 'Systema Naturae' and 'Species Plantarum', which contained vast classification systems for plants and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Hierarchical Classification System
keyAchievements.description: Introduced the three kingdoms of Animalia, Plantae, and Mineralia, along with ranks such as class, order, genus, species, and variety (and division/phylum).

keyAchievements.title: Proposal of the 'Economy of Nature' Concept
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the concept of the 'economy of nature' to explain the self-renewal and cyclical processes of nature, laying the foundation for ecological research.

faq.question: Who is Carl Linnaeus?
faq.answer: He was a Swedish botanist who made decisive contributions to establishing the foundations of biological taxonomy, often called the 'father of modern botany'.

faq.question: What is Carl Linnaeus's most important taxonomic achievement?
faq.answer: He established binomial nomenclature, which combines a genus name and a species name, and formalized it by consistently applying it in his works.

faq.question: How did Linnaeus classify living organisms?
faq.answer: He classified living organisms by introducing three kingdoms—Animal, Plant, and Mineral—and hierarchical ranks such as class, order, family, genus, species, and variety.

faq.question: Is Linnaeus's classification system still used today?
faq.answer: While Linnaean taxonomy became the cornerstone of modern taxonomy, it contained several errors, such as classifying plants based solely on their reproductive organs, and has been heavily modified since.

faq.question: What role did Linnaeus's students play?
faq.answer: His students, known as the 'Apostles', traveled the world on expeditions to collect new biological samples, contributing to the creation of Linnaeus's global taxonomy.

Dropped out of Hertford Grammar School due to family financial difficulties.
Left London and moved to Kington, near the Welsh border.
Worked as a surveyor in the West of England and Wales.

Explored jointly with botanist Richard Spruce and his younger brother Herbert.
Lost his collected specimens in a ship fire while returning to England on the 'Helen'.
Traveled through the Malay Archipelago and the East Indies, collecting specimens and studying nature.
Conceived the concept of evolution through natural selection independently of Charles Darwin.
Returned to England, organized his collections, and gave lectures.
Became interested in spiritualism.
Married Annie Mitten and had three children.
His wife Annie Mitten was diagnosed with stomach cancer due to alcoholism.
Published 'The Malay Archipelago', chronicling his research and explorations.

keyAchievements.title: Co-discoverer of Evolution by Natural Selection
keyAchievements.description: Independently conceived the concept of evolution by natural selection in 1858, which prompted Charles Darwin to publish 'On the Origin of Species'.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Wallace Line
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the Wallace Line, a faunal discontinuity separating the fauna of Asia and Oceania, during his explorations of the Amazon River basin and the Malay Archipelago.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Wallace Effect
keyAchievements.description: Significantly contributed to the development of 19th-century evolutionary theory by proposing the Wallace Effect, which explains warning coloration in animals and speciation.

keyAchievements.title: Father of Biogeography
keyAchievements.description: Often called the 'Father of Biogeography' for his contributions to the study of the relationship between animal species distribution and geography.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Malay Archipelago'
keyAchievements.description: 'The Malay Archipelago', which details his research and explorations, became one of the most famous books on 19th-century scientific exploration.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Extraterrestrial Life
keyAchievements.description: Made the first serious attempt to evaluate the possibility of life on other planets in his 1904 book 'Man's Place in the Universe', and was one of the first scientists to write about the search for life on Mars.

faq.question: Who is Alfred Russel Wallace?
faq.answer: He was a British naturalist, explorer, geographer, anthropologist, and biologist who independently conceived the theory of evolution through natural selection alongside Charles Darwin.

faq.question: What is the Wallace Line?
faq.answer: The Wallace Line is a boundary he discovered during his explorations in the Amazon basin and the Malay Archipelago that marks a sharp faunal discontinuity between the animal species of Asia and Oceania.

faq.question: What important books did Wallace write?
faq.answer: He authored books such as 'Palm Trees of the Amazon and Their Uses', 'A Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro', 'The Malay Archipelago', and 'Man's Place in the Universe'.

faq.question: Why is Wallace called the 'father of biogeography'?
faq.answer: He earned the title 'father of biogeography' for his contributions to the study of the relationship between the geographic distribution of animal species.

faq.question: What was the relationship between Alfred Russel Wallace and Charles Darwin?
faq.answer: Wallace independently formulated the concept of evolution by natural selection in 1858, which served as the catalyst for Charles Darwin to publish 'On the Origin of Species'.

timeline.event: Born as the eldest of 10 children in Kinnordy, Angus, Scotland.
Graduated from the University of Oxford.
Published his first paper, 'On a Recent Formation of Freshwater Limestone in Forfarshire'.
Became a Fellow of the Royal Society.
Abandoned his legal career to pursue geology full-time.
Appointed Professor of Geology at King's College London.
Authored the first three volumes of 'Principles of Geology'.
Traveled to the United States and Canada.
Published 'Travels in North America'.
Knighted (Knight Bachelor).
Published 'A Second Visit to the United States'.
Awarded the Copley Medal (Royal Society).
Created a Baronet.
Awarded the Wollaston Medal (Geological Society of London).
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the Percentage Method
keyAchievements.description: Together with Roderick Murchison, he studied the Italian marine Tertiary strata and completed the percentage method with the help of Gérard Paul Deshayes.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'Principles of Geology'
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Principles of Geology', which established the modern framework of geology and comprehensively explained geological phenomena under the concept that 'the present is the key to the past'.

keyAchievements.title: Popularization of Uniformitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Widely popularized uniformitarianism, a concept introduced by James Hutton, through his book 'Principles of Geology'.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote Travelogues Based on Geological Theory
keyAchievements.description: Based on his travel experiences in the US and Canada, he wrote travelogues incorporating geological theories, such as 'Travels in North America' and 'A Second Visit to the United States'.

faq.question: Who was Charles Lyell?
faq.answer: He was a British geologist who founded modern geology and is often called the father of geology.

faq.question: What is Charles Lyell's most famous work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is 'Principles of Geology', written between 1830 and 1833, which established the modern framework of geology and widely popularized uniformitarianism.

faq.question: How did Charles Lyell influence Charles Darwin?
faq.answer: Before the voyage of the Beagle, Charles Darwin received and eagerly read the first volume of Lyell's 'Principles of Geology', which greatly aided the development and emergence of Darwin's theory of evolution.

faq.question: What awards did Charles Lyell receive?
faq.answer: He received the Copley Medal in 1858 and the Wollaston Medal in 1866; he was knighted in 1848 and made a baronet in 1864.

timeline.event: Born near Farab in Central Asia.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and centered around Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Left Numerous Writings
keyAchievements.description: Left numerous writings, including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

keyAchievements.title: Multidisciplinary Scholar
keyAchievements.description: Specialized in science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

keyAchievements.title: First Iranian Philosopher of the Islamic Era
keyAchievements.description: He is the first Iranian philosopher to emerge in the Islamic era.

keyAchievements.title: Revered as the Second Teacher
keyAchievements.description: Revered as the 'Second Teacher' following Aristotle.

faq.question: Who was Al-Farabi?
faq.answer: He was an Islamic (Turkic) philosopher active from 872 to 950 who specialized in various fields including science, philosophy, logic, sociology, medicine, mathematics, and music.

faq.question: What are Al-Farabi's major achievements?
faq.answer: He established a philosophical system based on Aristotle and Neoplatonism, and left behind numerous works including 'Treatise on the Intellect' and 'On the Ideal City'.

faq.question: What was Al-Farabi's epithet?
faq.answer: He was revered as the 'Second Teacher', following Aristotle.

faq.question: Where was Al-Farabi born?
faq.answer: He was born near Farab in Central Asia.

faq.question: Which languages was Al-Farabi fluent in?
faq.answer: He was proficient in dozens of languages, including Arabic, Persian, and Turkic.

keyAchievements.title: Served as Lord Chancellor

keyAchievements.title: Martyrdom for his Beliefs

keyAchievements.title: Canonization as a Saint

faq.question: Who was Thomas More?
faq.answer: Thomas More was a lawyer, writer, thinker, statesman of the Kingdom of England, and a Christian saint.

faq.question: What is Thomas More's major work?
faq.answer: He authored 'Utopia' in 1516, which is the name he gave to an imaginary island nation possessing an ideal political system.

faq.question: Why was Thomas More executed?
faq.answer: He was executed for treason for refusing to accept Henry VIII's claim to be the Supreme Head of the Church of England.

faq.question: When was Thomas More canonized as a saint?
faq.answer: He was canonized by Pope Pius XI in 1935, 400 years after his death, and declared the patron saint of statesmen and politicians by Pope John Paul II in 2000.

faq.question: What was Thomas More's stance on the Reformation?
faq.answer: Thomas More opposed the Reformation, actively suppressed proponents of Western church reform, supported the Pope, and advocated for peace and social justice through the existing system.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of a Unique Philosophical System
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to combine realism and idealism, establishing a unique philosophy which argued that while the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith', certain knowledge of 'essences' is possible.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Major Book

keyAchievements.title: Coined Famous Aphorism

faq.question: What was Santayana's main philosophy?
faq.answer: Santayana held a unique philosophy attempting to combine realism and idealism, asserting that the existence of material reality is based on irrational 'animal faith' but arguing for certain knowledge of 'essences'. His thought is characterized by an attempt to merge robust naturalism with beautiful romanticism.

faq.question: What is Santayana's famous aphorism?
faq.answer: Santayana left the famous quote, 'Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.'

faq.question: What is Santayana's representative work?
faq.answer: Santayana's representative work is the novel 'The Last Puritan'.

faq.question: When was William Tyndale born?
faq.answer: William Tyndale was born in Gloucestershire in 1494.

faq.question: What are William Tyndale's main achievements?
faq.answer: He translated the Koine Greek Bible into English, completed and printed the English New Testament based on the original Hebrew and Greek texts. He also laid the foundation for the King James Bible.

faq.question: How did William Tyndale die?
faq.answer: He was arrested in 1535 on charges of translating the Bible into English and was burned at the stake as a heretic on October 6, 1536.

faq.question: What languages could William Tyndale speak?
faq.answer: He could speak seven languages: English, Hebrew, Greek, Latin, Italian, Spanish, and French.

faq.question: What was William Tyndale's relationship with Thomas More?
faq.answer: In 1531, William Tyndale harshly criticized Thomas More by writing 'An Answer unto Sir Thomas More's Dialogue', a rebuttal to More's 'A Dialogue Concerning Heresies'.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Post-Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: A French Post-Impressionist painter who served as a bridge between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a New Painting Language
keyAchievements.description: Created a new painting language by altering traditional perspective and emphasizing the fundamental structure and formal characteristics of objects.

keyAchievements.title: Color Plane and Brushstroke Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Characterized by unique and exploratory brushwork, using color planes and small brushstrokes to form complex fields.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Geometric Forms
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the pictorial idea of treating nature in terms of simple geometric shapes like the cylinder, sphere, and cone, and placing objects within perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Expression of Depth through Binocular Vision
keyAchievements.description: Explored binocular vision to express visual perception of the same phenomenon, providing a new aesthetic experience of depth.

keyAchievements.title: Montagne Sainte-Victoire Series
keyAchievements.description: Demonstrated precise observation of nature and pictorial exploration through his series on Montagne Sainte-Victoire in his hometown of Aix-en-Provence.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: Provided decisive insight and inspiration to early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, including Picasso and Matisse, earning him the title 'the father of us all'.

faq.question: What kind of painter was Paul Cézanne?
faq.answer: He was a French Post-Impressionist painter who bridged late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.

faq.question: How were Cézanne's works received during his lifetime?
faq.answer: His paintings were initially misunderstood and ridiculed by contemporary art critics, but they were re-evaluated after his first solo exhibition in 1895, and in his later years, he was revered as a master by younger artists.

faq.question: What was Cézanne's impact on modern art?
faq.answer: He introduced new modes of expression that influenced early 20th-century avant-garde art movements, notably providing decisive inspiration to Cubist and Fauvist painters like Picasso and Matisse.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Cézanne's painting technique?
faq.answer: He altered traditional perspective, emphasized the fundamental structure and formal traits of objects, and pursued geometric simplification, treating nature as cylinders, spheres, and cones.

faq.answer: Auguste Rodin was born in Paris, France, on November 12, 1840.

faq.question: What are Auguste Rodin's representative works?
faq.answer: His representative works include 'The Burghers of Calais', 'The Gates of Hell', and 'The Thinker'.

faq.question: What impact did Auguste Rodin have on the world of sculpture?
faq.answer: He broke the stereotypes of sculpture and elevated the perception of sculpture to the same level as painting, and is thus considered the founder of modern sculpture.

faq.question: When did Auguste Rodin pass away?
faq.answer: He passed away on November 17, 1917.

faq.question: Which musical movement is Claude Debussy a representative composer of?
faq.answer: Claude Debussy is a representative composer of French Impressionist music.

faq.question: What are Debussy's major orchestral works?
faq.answer: Debussy's major orchestral works include 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune', 'Nocturnes' (1897-1899), and 'Images' (1905-1912).

faq.question: When was Debussy born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Debussy was born on August 22, 1862, and died on March 25, 1918.

faq.question: What education did Debussy receive, and what awards did he win?
faq.answer: He entered the Paris Conservatoire at the age of 10 and won the first prize in the Prix de Rome in 1884 with his cantata 'L'enfant prodigue'.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Debussy's music?
faq.answer: Debussy's music originated from a reaction against Wagner and the German musical tradition, seeking new forms instead of the classical symphony format.

faq.question: Which literary movement influenced Debussy?
faq.answer: Debussy was strongly influenced by the late 19th-century Symbolist poetry movement.

faq.question: In what language did Kafka write his works?
faq.answer: With the exception of a few letters written in Czech to Milena Jesenská, his works were mostly written in Prague German.

faq.question: How did Kafka want his works to be handled?
faq.answer: In his final testament sent to his friend facing his death, he requested that all his writings be burned.

faq.question: How did Kafka's works become known after his death?
faq.answer: His friend Max Brod defied Kafka's dying wish and oversaw the publication of many of the works he possessed, making them known worldwide.

faq.question: What kind of writer was James Joyce?
faq.answer: He was a writer from Dublin, Ireland, who worked in various genres including novels, poetry, and plays, and is recognized as a leading novelist of modernism.

faq.question: What are his representative works?
faq.answer: His famous novels include 'Ulysses', 'Finnegans Wake', 'Dubliners', and 'A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man'.

faq.question: What was the main setting that influenced his literary world?
faq.answer: His spiritual and fictional world is deeply rooted in his hometown of Dublin, Ireland, which provided much of the subject matter and settings for his novels.

faq.question: What was James Joyce's stance on the Catholic religion?
faq.answer: Through his Jesuit education, he learned about the injustices and cruelty of priests, leading him to distance himself from the Catholic religion and portray it critically in his works.

faq.question: Which of Puccini's arias are widely known?

faq.question: When did Puccini die?

faq.question: Who completed Puccini's unfinished opera 'Turandot'?

faq.question: What was Charles Baudelaire's profession?
faq.answer: He was a French critic and poet.

faq.question: What is Charles Baudelaire's most famous work?
faq.answer: His most famous work is his first poetry collection, 'Les Fleurs du mal' (The Flowers of Evil), published in 1857.

faq.question: What controversy surrounded the publication of 'Les Fleurs du mal'?
faq.answer: 'Les Fleurs du mal' was prosecuted for an offense against public morals for being indecent, resulting in a fine, but it was acquitted in 1949.

faq.question: Which author's works did Charles Baudelaire translate?
faq.answer: He translated the works of Edgar Allan Poe, whose short stories had a profound influence on him.

faq.question: What artistic movement did Charles Baudelaire influence?
faq.answer: He was praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine, and revered as the father of Symbolism.
